metadata.title: Dry Rot and Timber Decay in British Scientific Discourse, 1665-1830
metadata.description: Knowledge graph tracing the emergence of dry rot (Serpula lacrymans) awareness in British science, with evidence from the Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society and Pepys's Memoires (1690)
metadata.hypothesis: Serpula lacrymans arrived in Europe and spread aggressively in Britain during the second half of the 18th century, reaching crisis levels by the 1770s-1800s. It was almost certainly a major contributor to the naval dry rot crisis — Knowles explicitly names it destroying English oak ships, and the same organism devastating buildings was clearly entering ships through their softwood components. Green (unseasoned) timber forced into service under wartime pressure created ideal conditions. BUT the naval crisis was multi-causal and multi-organismal. Sowerby's 1812 dockyard survey documented ~25 fungal species stratified by humidity and substrate: (1) Xylostroma giganteum on oak in the saturated hold (800-900°); (2) Serpula lacrymans and Fibroporia vaillantii (both conifer specialists) on pine/deal between decks (700-800°); (3) numerous other species across dockyard timber. His 'very little remains' of B. lachrymans on the QC partly reflects removal of the most damaged softwood before his inspection, partly reflects the saturated hold conditions favouring wet-environment fungi over the damp-environment Serpula. The crisis = Serpula invasion + native decay fungi + green timber + poor ventilation + wartime shortcuts. No single organism explains it.
primary_sources: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society (1665-1869), 8,128 articles
Pepys, Memoires Relating to the State of the Royal Navy of England (1690)
Sowerby, Reports and Correspondence with the Navy Board (1812-1814), 34pp manuscript + 442pp NHM archive
Lambert, The Last Sailing Battlefleet (1991), Chapter 3: Timber
House of Lords Select Committee on Timber Trade (1820), 111pp
recent_updates: 2026-04-09: Added Burke 1791 (Thoughts on French Affairs) — earliest known metaphorical/political use of "dry rot," composed Dec 1791, published 1797.

organisms.name: Serpula lacrymans
diagnostic_features: Produces brown cubical rot reducing wood to dry dust (shared with native brown rot fungi)
UNIQUE: Grows thick rhizomorphs that transport water from damp areas to dry timber
UNIQUE: Spreads across brick, masonry, and dry gaps to find new wood
UNIQUE: Attacks timber that appears dry — the paradox that gave it its English name
Produces white/brown mycelial strands and cottony growth on wood surfaces
Fruiting body: orange-brown, wrinkled, with water droplets (lachrymans = weeping)
Infectious — spreads in ways that seemed inexplicable before the organism was understood
organisms.key_distinction: The symptom (brown cubical rot, dry powder) is shared with multiple native European fungi. What distinguishes Serpula is its BEHAVIOUR: spreading to dry timber via rhizomorphs. This is what made it uniquely destructive and what prompted the coining of the term 'dry rot' — rot where there shouldn't be rot.

arrival_europe.divergence_estimate: 2,000-5,000 years ago (molecular clock, Kauserud et al. 2007)
arrival_europe.uncertainty_window: Divergence date is ancient but European EXPANSION is post-medieval, associated with enclosed timber construction
arrival_europe.key_study: Kauserud et al. (2007) 'Asian origin and rapid global spread of the destructive dry rot fungus Serpula lacrymans', Molecular Ecology 16(16): 3350-3360. European populations show reduced genetic diversity consistent with founder event from Asian wild populations.
arrival_europe.wild_habitat: Montane coniferous forests at 2,000-4,000m elevation in NE India/Himalayas, Yunnan, Japan. Optimum temperature ~20-23°C.
arrival_europe.notes: The divergence between wild Asian and domestic European strains may be ancient, but the catastrophic expansion in European buildings dates to the 18th century. The organism may have been present at low levels before becoming a crisis — consistent with the evidence trajectory from Hales (1755) to the 1812 naval crisis.

organisms.name: Native European brown rot fungi

species.name: Coniophora puteana
species.notes: One of the most common causes of wet rot in European buildings. Also widespread in forests on dead conifer and broadleaf wood. Causes dark brown cubical rot; dried wood crumbles to powder identical to dry rot.

species.name: Laetiporus sulphureus
species.notes: Common bracket fungus on living and dead hardwoods, especially oaks. Attacks heartwood causing severe brown cubical rot. Advanced decay reduces heartwood to powder.

species.name: Fibroporia vaillantii
historical_synonyms: Boletus vaillantii DC. (1815) — basionym of accepted name
species.nomenclatural_chain: Boletus hybridus → Polyporus hybridus → Poria hybrida → heterotypic synonym of Fibroporia vaillantii (Parmasto, 1968). Confirmed by GBIF, MycoDB, Index Fungorum, MycoBank, and Petersen (1983) Mycotaxon 17: 148-230.
species.substrate: Coniferous wood specialist (spruce, pine, fir). No published record on oak specifically.
species.substrate_paradox: Sowerby found B. hybridus on HMS Queen Charlotte (nominally an oak ship), but Royal Navy ships used mixed timbers — pine/fir for decking, masts, interior joinery, ceiling planks. F. vaillantii likely colonized softwood components. Sowerby never explicitly says B. hybridus was on oak — he says it was on the ship.
species.sowerby_description: White rugged soft Fungi — young state of B. medulla-panis EF 326. White cottony mycelium forming dense membranes, thin flexible white strands, resupinate fruiting bodies with small angular pores.
species.abundance_on_qc: Present (listed first in formal report); 'G.C.' in B98 full species list
species.b98_draft_context: Sowerby explicitly distinguished ship fungi (B. hybridus + Xylostroma) from building dry rot (B. lachrymans 'found more in damp building on shore')
species.humidity_threshold: Not assigned a specific threshold in Sowerby's humidity-fungi scale
species.notes: Native to European pine forests. Causes brown rot degrading cellulose while leaving modified lignin. Berkeley (c.1860) described it as: 'White, mycelium thick, forming a dense membrane, or creeping branched strings, hymenium breaking up into areæ, pores long, slender, minute.' 1911 Encyclopædia Britannica: 'Polyporus hybridus, which thrives in oak-built ships.' Polish building surveys: 13.8% of decay cases. Norwegian roof beams: genus Antrodia most frequent at 17.3%.

species.name: Fomitopsis pinicola
species.notes: One of the most common wood-decaying polypores in European coniferous forests. Highly efficient brown rot fungus.

species.name: Donkioporia expansa
historical_synonyms: Boletus expansus Desm. (1823) — basionym, SEPARATE from B. hybridus
species.nomenclatural_note: Was NEVER connected nomenclaturally to Boletus hybridus. The KG previously suggested B. hybridus might be D. expansa — this is incorrect. The two have entirely separate basionyms and synonym chains.
species.rot_type: White rot (degrades both lignin AND cellulose) — produces pale, stringy, fibrous wood, NOT brown cubical cracking
species.substrate: Oak specialist — 'can cause more extensive damage to oak than any other fungus' (BCS Preservation)
species.death_watch_beetle: Critical ecological partnership with Xestobium rufovillosum (death watch beetle). Female beetles preferentially oviposit on oak pre-decayed by D. expansa (Belmain et al. 2002).
species.notes: Major cause of wet rot in oak structural timber. Requires persistently wet conditions. Mycelium flat, firmly adherent, does not spread far — poor morphological match to Sowerby's description of B. hybridus ('White rugged soft Fungi' with 'broad ramifications'). Despite being the modern oak decay specialist, it was not what Sowerby called B. hybridus.

diagnostic_features: Require sustained high moisture to grow — stop spreading when wood dries out
Produce brown cubical rot identical in appearance to Serpula lacrymans
Advanced decay reduces wood to dry powder indistinguishable from dry rot symptom
Produce fruiting bodies (toadstools, brackets) in damp conditions
Do NOT transport water through mycelium — cannot attack dry timber
Cannot spread across masonry, brick, or dry gaps
Native to European forests — not invasive species
organisms.key_distinction_from_serpula: The symptom (brown cubical rot, dry powder) is identical. The BEHAVIOUR is different: native brown rot fungi require sustained dampness and stop when wood dries. Serpula lacrymans spreads via rhizomorphs to dry timber, across masonry, attacking wood that appears dry. The name 'dry rot' describes this paradoxical behaviour, not the powder symptom.
organisms.notes: The organisms Pepys observed in 1680s ship holds — large toadstools in damp, unventilated conditions, timber 'perished to powder' — are fully explained by native brown rot fungi without requiring Serpula lacrymans to be present.

ship_decay_ecology.title: Three-Organism Stratified Model of Naval Ship Decay
ship_decay_ecology.description: Naval 'dry rot' was a multi-organism syndrome stratified by humidity zone and timber substrate within ships. Sowerby's 1812 humidity-fungi scale and the mixed-timber construction of warships explain why different organisms dominated different parts of the vessel.
ship_decay_ecology.note_on_sowerby_inspection: IMPORTANT CAVEAT: Sowerby inspected the QC after 'great quantities of Timber had been removed.' The between-decks zone (pine ceiling planks, deal decking) — where B. lachrymans would have been most prevalent — was the most accessible area and likely stripped first. His 'very little remains' of B. lachrymans may partly reflect this removal bias, not just ecological absence. The Navy Board cited this stripping to deny him credit.

zones.timber: Oak frames, keelson, heavy structural members
zones.notes: Sowerby ranked Xylostroma as the most destructive: 'with a gigantic stride... rending it in all directions to a mere powder.' His humidity scale places it at 800-900°. 'A Gigantic Leather-like Fungus peculiar to rotten Oak.' Fine specimens in the Master builders counting house at Plymouth. The QC hold at 828.6° was squarely in this zone.

zones.zone: Between decks / above waterline (damp, not wet)
zones.timber: Pine/fir ceiling planks, deal decking, interior joinery
zones.notes: This zone is ecologically analogous to buildings — enclosed, damp but not saturated, with softwood substrate. Both B. lachrymans and B. hybridus are conifer specialists thriving in this humidity range. Sowerby's scale places B. lachrymans at 700-800°. This is also the zone most accessible to repair crews — and therefore the zone most likely already stripped when Sowerby inspected. His 'very little remains' may partly reflect removal of the most damaged pine/deal from this area before his arrival.
Sowerby's safe range. Fungi do not grow below 500° in most conditions. His recommendation to ventilate and use hygrometers was aimed at keeping the between-decks zone in this range.

ship_timber_composition.source: Primarily from Knowles 1821 Chapter IX (pp.142-180), supplemented by Sowerby, Pering, NAUTICUS, and Blackburn

english_oak.uses: Frames, hull planking, stems, stern-posts, port timbers — the primary structural timber
english_oak.source_regions: English forests; declining supply — 'not one quarter part of the oak timber in the royal forests that there was two centuries ago' (Blackburn 1817)
english_oak.durability: Longest-lasting of British timbers when properly seasoned. Royal William lasted 94 years (mostly in harbour). Royal Sovereign (1636) 'perfectly sound' after 50 years, 'timber so firm and hard as almost to resist the edge of tools' (Knowles p.181). But declining: 30 years (17th C) → 14 years (early 18th C) → 8 years (Napoleonic wars).
english_oak.sowerby_assessment: 'May become nearly as hard as Iron by age and proper attention.' English oak especially valued over foreign oak.
english_oak.vulnerability: Attacked by Xylostroma giganteum in saturated conditions (hold). When green/unseasoned, vulnerable to all decay. Knowles: ships of English oak 'only' still destroyed by B. lachrymans. Sap is the weak point: 'the fungi attacks the sap, and by that means obtains access to, and destroys the heart' (Blackburn p.4466).

canada_oak.uses: Frames and structural timber — used as substitute for English oak during timber shortage
canada_oak.source_regions: Canada, transported down the St. Lawrence
canada_oak.durability: Far less durable than English oak. Knowles p.117: QC's defects 'ascribed to the great quantities of Canada oak... woods, particularly the former, subject to be soon decomposed.' NAUTICUS: 'the existence of this sort of American oak never exceeds four or five years.'
canada_oak.vulnerability: The most vulnerable oak type. Contact with Canada oak was believed to infect adjacent English oak timbers.

german_oak.uses: Building and repairs — imported from Holstein 1772 and 1802
german_oak.durability: Disastrous. 'Presented a fair outside, which was a mere shell of about one-fourth of an inch in thickness, the inside was found to be completely decomposed.' HMS Blake (launched 1808, unserviceable 1813) and HMS St. Domingo (launched 1809, unserviceable 1814) both built of Holstein oak. 'Its further importation has been discontinued.' (Knowles p.2287)
german_oak.vulnerability: Decomposed from inside out within 4-5 years.

adriatic_oak.durability: Variable: 'some being of a hard and good quality, and part having a more soft and open grain' (Knowles p.2289)

dantzic_oak_plank.uses: Bottoms of ships (below waterline), lower deck planking, gun-deck planking. 'Used for centuries past.'
dantzic_oak_plank.source_regions: Poland, via Dantzic (Gdansk). Cut in winter, floated down to port.
dantzic_oak_plank.durability: Extremely durable underwater: 'generally when most of the timbers of ships are decayed, their bottoms are sound... commonly wears out two sets of frames.' Quality graded by Prussian Braakers: K (crown/best), W (single-braaked), WW (double-braaked).
dantzic_oak_plank.price: ~£15.15s per load (£6 duty + £9.15s cost). Pepys's 1686 conference of master builders: 'foreign plank is more durable, and every way better for the building of large ships than the English.'

american_pitch_pine.uses: Ceiling planks (interior lining), between-decks components, some structural applications
american_pitch_pine.durability: Most vulnerable to fungi of all ship timbers. NAUTICUS: 'this wood is the soonest destroyed by fungi, as these plants are nourished by the great quantity of resin contained in its numerous cells.' Sowerby: turpentine veins confine moisture, creating ideal fungal habitat. Blackburn: 'the pitch-pine of Carolina' — dry rot is 'the natural decay' of this timber.
american_pitch_pine.sowerby_assessment: Directed pitch pine to be 'perfectly cleared of every vestige of decay' and thoroughly dried before reuse.
american_pitch_pine.vulnerability: Primary food source for Serpula lacrymans and Fibroporia vaillantii. Fungi grow specifically in the resin cells (NAUTICUS). Between-decks location places it in the 700-800° humidity zone where Serpula thrives.
american_pitch_pine.knowles_b95: 'It is not only in this ship that Pitch-pine has proved more subject to dry rot than other wood, but also in the Bellepoule Frigate... timber grown in America is very subject to decomposition from that cause.'

canada_white_yellow_pine.species: Pinus strobus (Eastern White Pine / Weymouth Pine)
canada_white_yellow_pine.nomenclature_note: Canadian yellow pine and white pine are the SAME species (Pinus strobus). The British market took time to settle on 'white pine' as the standard name; Knowles (1821) and Lingard (1819) still use both terms. Knowles groups 'red and yellow pine-timber' together (p.2517) as Canadian imports after 1807, but separately builds '3 frigates of the white pine' (p.2519) — likely the same species under different names.
canada_white_yellow_pine.uses_masts: Known in dockyards long before its use in construction — sourced from New England for masts dating back to before the American Revolution. The 'Broad Arrow' policy reserved the tallest white pines for Royal Navy masts. Well-known to dockyard men as a mast timber, but NOT for ship construction until the timber crisis forced its adoption after 1807.
canada_white_yellow_pine.uses_construction: Shipbuilding and repairs — 3 frigates built of white pine 1814-1815. Also used for repairs alongside red pine after large-scale Canadian imports began 1807.
canada_white_yellow_pine.source_regions: Canada (formerly New England before American independence). Imported in large quantities after 1807 when Berlin/Milan decrees closed European ports to British commerce.
canada_white_yellow_pine.durability: Average less than 3 years for frigates — the worst performance of all ship timbers. Knowles p.2519: 'three of the white pine... the average durability of the latter, rather less than three years.'
canada_white_yellow_pine.lingard_1819: p.172: 'In the red and yellow pine, the increase of strength has, on experiment, been found equivalent to one sixth' — yellow pine weaker than red, could be improved with preservative treatment.
canada_white_yellow_pine.vulnerability: Soft, light, porous — ideal substrate for both Serpula lacrymans and Fibroporia vaillantii. Its reintroduction from mast timber to construction timber during the Napoleonic crisis put the most fungus-vulnerable wood into the most fungus-prone locations.

canada_red_pine.uses: Decking and shipbuilding — 15 frigates built of red pine in 1814-1815. Proved useful for decking and continued in use throughout the first half of the 19th century.
canada_red_pine.source_regions: Canada, imported in large quantities after 1807
canada_red_pine.durability: Average 3.5 years for frigates (Knowles p.2519). HMS Halifax (sloop, 1806, built of birch, red-pine, and oak) — pine 'totally decayed' by 1812. Better than white pine but still far short of oak or Baltic fir.
canada_red_pine.knowles_1821: p.2519: 'fifteen frigates were built of the red... during the years 1814 and 1815; the average durability of the former, was three years and a half'
canada_red_pine.notes: Despite poor initial performance in frigates, red pine found a lasting role for decking — a use where regular replacement was expected and its properties were adequate.

dantzic_deals.uses: Weather-decks (exposed upper decks). 'Used for centuries past for the weather-decks of ships.'
dantzic_deals.durability: 'Better adapted than any others for this purpose' (Knowles). Exposed to weather = regular wetting and drying.

baltic_fir.uses: Frigates and sloops — entire hulls built of fir from 1757. Dantzic and Riga sources.
baltic_fir.durability: Average 8-9 years. Riga fir 'considered superior in durability' to Dantzic fir. First fir frigates: 5 × 28-gun ships in 1757. Six frigates built 1796, six more 1804-1805. 'Quite as long as frigates built in haste of oak.'
baltic_fir.sowerby_assessment: 'Deal of any kind is always comparatively soft and spongy... very apt to absorb water... Its being splintery renders it an unmerciful act to use more than can be helped in Ships of war.'

elm.uses: Keel, garboard strakes (lowest hull planks). Also shells of blocks, dead-eyes, pumps.
elm.durability: 'Durable only where constantly under water or in dry situation; alternately wet and dry, very soon decays.' (Knowles p.2191)

beech.uses: Garboard strake and lower hull planks, particularly amidships
beech.durability: 'Durable only in situations where it is not subject to frequent changes' — same as elm. (Knowles p.2193)

larch_scottish.uses: Experimental — HMS Athole (28 guns) built entirely of larch, launched Nov 1820. Also used in Serapis store-ship, Sybille frigate.
larch_scottish.source_regions: Duke of Atholl's estate at Dunkeld, Scotland. 12,984 English acres planted by 1820.
larch_scottish.durability: 'No appearance of lichen or fungi growing upon any of it' when stored in dockyard piles. Red and white varieties. Tenacious quality — auger produces 'almost solid' core. Light (34 lbs/cubic foot seasoned). 'Slow of combustion, and is said to let shot through without splintering.'
larch_scottish.notes: A promising new timber. Venice built on larch piles. Duke of Atholl's ferry-boat sound after 23 years.

teak.uses: Ships built at Bombay dockyard — frames, structural timber. HMS Minden (74 guns, 1810) first line-of-battle ship.
teak.durability: 'No definite period can be fixed' — essentially indefinite. Greater strength than English oak at lower specific gravity. Minden 'now in excellent condition' after 11 years of active service.
teak.notes: Duplicate teak frames shipped to England for assembly. Some evidence of preserving iron; other cases of destroying iron bolts.

mahogany.uses: Large structural parts where iron not convenient — alternative to oak
mahogany.sowerby_assessment: 'Some kinds of which I understand are now cheap, are good materials for the large & strong parts of Ships where Iron is not convenient.'

chestnut.uses: Occasionally passed off as oak. 'Received as such, and worked into ships; when this has been the case, it has been discovered in a short time by its being in a decayed state.' Detection: water on saw — oak stains black (gallic acid + iron), chestnut does not.

stink_wood.uses: Imported from Cape of Good Hope
stink_wood.durability: 'Soon decomposed and subject to the fungus rot. Its further importation has not been encouraged.' (Knowles p.2353)

implications: The substrate paradox for B. hybridus = F. vaillantii disappears: both F. vaillantii and S. lacrymans are conifer specialists and both were on the pine/fir components between decks, not on the oak frames

Sowerby's 'found more in damp building on shore' (B98 p.16) is not saying B. lachrymans was absent from ships — it's saying it favours the damp (not wet) environment characteristic of buildings. The damp-not-wet zone of a ship IS ecologically similar to a building interior.
The three organisms occupied distinct but overlapping niches, stratified by humidity and substrate within the same vessel
The term 'dry rot' was applied to all three organisms' effects, but only Serpula lacrymans exhibits the distinctive spreading-to-dry-timber behaviour that originally named the disease

earliest_known_use.usage: Named condition of naval rope — fibres becoming brittle and powdery. Paired with 'Mildew' as 'the Two great Enemies' of naval stores. Term existed for material decay decades before application to timber.

first_application_to_timber.context: buildings — church floors, pews, structural timbers
first_application_to_timber.notes: Earliest confirmed use applied to TIMBER. 'a dry Rot has destroyed the Floors and Pews, and likewise the Timbers which support the Building.' In Parliamentary records during Pringle's presidency (1772-1778). Confirms the 1823 Britannica claim.

etymology.notes: The TERM existed by 1725 for rope decay. Its APPLICATION TO TIMBER dates to the 1770s-1780s and likely coincides with the spread of Serpula lacrymans. The 1823 Britannica claim that the disease 'was not known... by its present name, beyond the middle of the last century' may refer specifically to the timber application, not the term itself. UPDATE 2026-04: Burke's metaphorical use in Thoughts on French Affairs (composed Dec 1791, pub 1797) shows the term had crossed into political prose by 1791, confirming wide circulation a generation before the naval crisis.

first_phil_trans_use.usage: casual, undefined — implies term already in common use for timber

earliest_periodical_discussion.usage: fully established disease name; public debate about causes and cures

terminology.britannica_1823_claim: not known by its present name beyond the middle of the last century — likely refers to the TIMBER application, not the term itself which existed earlier for cordage

earliest_metaphorical_use.author: Edmund Burke
earliest_metaphorical_use.context: political — Jacobin subversion as hidden internal decay
earliest_metaphorical_use.significance: Earliest known non-technical use; shows the term had reached elite political vocabulary by 1791, fifteen years before the Queen Charlotte and twenty before Knowles dates the term's popularisation

first_phil_trans_use.usage: descriptive adjective, NOT the disease name

terminology.notes: Dominant framework for understanding all organic decay; applied to timber via miasmatic theory
Used for wood disease in trees (Knight 1795); parallel vocabulary to dry rot but for living trees
Marine worms (Teredo) destroying ship hulls — distinct from fungal rot but sometimes conflated in early sources
Generic term encompassing all forms of wood deterioration

first_use.usage: Pepys gathered them with his own hands — fruiting bodies of wet rot fungi

terminology.notes: Pepys's toadstools are the earliest documented observation of fungal fruiting bodies on British naval timber
The Commissioners' language for timber decay — treated as an infection that might have 'remains or returns' but without any specific disease name

terminology.term: perish'd to powder / reduc'd to powder

uses.context: ship timber in harbour — damp conditions, likely native brown rot fungi
Commissioners' report — 'even in their Thickest stuff'
dry rot definition — 'reduces it to a mass of dry dust'

terminology.notes: This symptom is NOT exclusive to Serpula lacrymans. Multiple native European brown rot fungi produce identical brown cubical rot and dry powder: Coniophora puteana (cellar fungus), Laetiporus sulphureus (chicken of the woods), Fibroporia vaillantii (mine fungus), Fomitopsis pinicola (red-belted conk). All digest cellulose while leaving lignin, causing cubical cracking and powdering. The distinguishing feature of Serpula is not the symptom but the BEHAVIOUR: spreading to dry timber via rhizomorphs. Pepys's 'perished to powder' in damp ship holds is fully explained by native brown rot fungi.
Proposed as more accurate name than 'dry rot'

people.name: Sir John Pringle
people.relevance: Authority on putrefaction; 1823 Britannica claims dry rot was known during his presidency

people.name: Stephen Hales
people.relevance: First to explicitly link ship timber decay to confined air (1755); proposed ventilation as remedy

people.name: Charles Stanhope, 3rd Earl Stanhope (Lord Viscount Mahon)
people.relevance: First to use term 'dry-rot' in Phil Trans (1778)

people.name: James Cook
people.relevance: Reported methods for keeping ships dry and healthy to Pringle (1776)

people.name: Joseph Priestley
people.relevance: Researched putrid air and its effects; addressed findings to Pringle

people.name: William Watson
people.relevance: 1742 observations on ship ventilation linking putrid air to both health and decay

people.name: David Macbride
people.relevance: Tanning (anti-putrefaction of organic matter); paper communicated by Pringle 1778

people.name: Edward King
people.relevance: 1779 paper on material preservation (iron solutions) addressed to Pringle

people.name: Henry Miles
people.relevance: 1749 microscopic observation of fungus on firewood — British scientists could observe wood-decay fungi but didn't connect them to timber disease

people.name: Anthony van Leeuwenhoek
people.relevance: 1694 observations on timber quality and unexpected rot — attributed to pre-existing 'inward decay', not post-harvest fungal attack

people.name: Henry Smeathman
people.relevance: 1781 account of termites destroying wooden structures in tropics — biological destruction of wood, but insects not fungi

people.name: Robert Adam
people.relevance: Built the Adelphi complex (1768-1772) which later suffered dry rot (c.1800). His firm (Mell. Adams) was sued by George Keate for dry rot in a new building (1785, Times). Sir William Chambers and Banks testified. The Society of Arts, housed in the Adelphi, offered a gold medal for solving dry rot (1783). The Adams are at the centre of the early building dry rot crisis.

people.name: Sir William Chambers
people.relevance: Arbitrated the Keate v. Adams dry rot case (1785). Found no cause of action — declared the Adams had built with 'great professional skill and ability.' One of Britain's leading architects directly involved in adjudicating a dry rot dispute.

people.name: David Hartley (the Younger)
people.relevance: 1776 fire-plates pamphlet confirms 'Dry-Rot' was 'commonly called' by that date. Admits 'we do not know the real cause.' Notes the wainscot puzzle — dry rot in open rooms contradicts ventilation theory. Proposes renaming it 'Wet-Rot.' Mann (1778) cites his fireproofing experiments in the same text that introduces 'Dry-rot' to French readers. Parliament voted him £2,500 for fireproofing experiments (1774-1776). George III visited his experimental house on Putney Heath.

people.name: Théodore Augustin Mann (l'Abbé Mann)
people.role: Benedictine monk, scientist, FRS, member of the Imperial Academy of Sciences at Brussels
people.relevance: Introduced the English term 'Dry-rot' to a French scientific audience in October 1778. Connected to Banks and the Royal Society. Published in Phil Trans 1779 (Rivers and Canals, communicated by Banks). Shows international circulation of dry rot knowledge.

people.name: Sir Joseph Banks
people.relevance: Testified as expert witness on dry rot and wood decay in the Keate v. Adams trial (Dec 1785). Banks was PRS during the entire dry rot crisis but NO Phil Trans articles on dry rot appeared during his 42-year presidency. He knew about it — testified in court about it — but it never entered the Transactions. Also a member of the Society for the Encouragement of Arts, Manufactures and Commerce in an early period and again after 1791 — but NOT during the critical years (1783-1789+) when the Society was offering its gold medal for discovering the cause of dry rot. Banks was absent from the one institution actively seeking a solution while simultaneously being the expert witness on the problem in court.

society_of_arts_membership.notes: Absent during the gold medal dry rot prize years (1783-1789+)

people.name: Thomas Andrew Knight
people.relevance: 1795 observations on 'canker' disease in fruit trees — wood 'soft and spongy'; fungal pathology described without mycological vocabulary

people.name: William Withering
people.relevance: First British botanist to include B. lachrymans ('dry-rot') in a standard flora (1792, 2nd ed.). Absent from his 1776 1st edition — brackets the recognition of Serpula to 1776-1792. Sowerby criticised him for never having seen the plant personally.

people.name: James Sowerby
people.role: Naturalist, botanical illustrator, mycologist; Navy Board consultant on dry rot (1812-1814)
people.relevance: Published first British mycological illustrations and descriptions of dry rot fungi (1797). Tab. CXIII illustrates B. lachrymans as 'Common Dry-rot'. Then in 1812, HIRED BY THE NAVY BOARD to inspect HMS Queen Charlotte at Plymouth — the same man who illustrated the organism 15 years earlier. Inspected the ship 24-25 July 1812, identified specific fungi by his own English Fungi plate numbers. Crucially found 'very little remains of Boletus Lachrymans E.F. 113' but abundant B. hybridus EF 289 and Xylostroma giganteum EF 358 — supporting a two-organism distinction between ship and building dry rot. His recommendations (ventilation, hygrometers, seasoned timber, NO chemical treatments) were adopted fleet-wide by the Admiralty on 28 Aug 1812. Subsequently inspected Deptford and Woolwich dockyards with Seppings (Oct 1813), commissioned to build 6 experimental timber stacks at Deptford (Nov 1813), and awarded 200 guineas (Jan 1814). His archive (NHM B95-B98) contains 442 pages of correspondence, notebooks, drafts, and fair copies documenting the entire engagement.

people.name: Franz Xaver von Wulfen
people.relevance: First to classify Serpula lacrymans (1781)
people.notes: Classification outside Phil Trans; suggests organism was being encountered by European naturalists by late 18th century

people.name: Robert M'William
people.relevance: Author of 'An Essay on the Origin and Operation of the Dry Rot' (London, 1818) — listed in Phil Trans Back Matter 1819
people.notes: Part of the explosion of dry rot literature post-1800

people.name: John Knowles
people.role: Navy Office official; author of dry rot book (1821)
people.relevance: Secretary to the Committee of Surveyors of His Majesty's Navy — insider with access to records from 1661. CLAIMS CREDIT for recommending Sowerby's engagement: 'It was at the recommendation of the author, that that eminent botanist, Mr. Sowerby, was employed.' Appears in the Sowerby archive (B95) as Navy Office correspondent (41 pages, 1812-1815). Sent with Sir William Rule to Plymouth to oversee implementation. His 1821 book is the fullest account of the Queen Charlotte affair from the Navy's perspective. Provides the critical dating: 'dry-rot' entered official naval documents only in 1808. Confirms A. Bowden won the Society of Arts gold medal for dry rot in 1818.

people.name: Samuel Pepys
people.role: Secretary of the Admiralty, FRS, PRS (1684-1686)
people.relevance: Gathered toadstools 'as big as my Fists' from ship holds in the 1680s — first documented observation of fungal fruiting bodies on British naval timber. Diagnosed cause as neglect (lack of ventilation, graving, breaming), not disease. The conditions described are wet rot, not dry rot.
people.notes: Pepys's Memoires (1690) provides the critical baseline for what timber decay looked like BEFORE Serpula lacrymans arrived

people.name: Samuel Sutton
people.relevance: Invented ship ventilation system (1742) — uses ship's galley fire to draw foul air from hold

people.name: Richard Mead
people.relevance: Communicated Sutton's ventilation invention to RS (1742)

people.name: Lionel Lukin
people.role: Inventor, timber preservation specialist; Navy consultant
people.relevance: Accompanied Sowerby to Plymouth to inspect HMS Queen Charlotte (July 1812). Had previously given 'many' reports to the Navy Board on dry rot. Employed ventilation methods with success. In 1811 erected a kiln at Woolwich dockyard for timber preservation — a fatal explosion killed 6 workers. Sowerby credits Lukin for pointing out the rubbish-in-hollows problem aboard HMS San Domingo. Lukin is the practical Navy man; Sowerby is the scientific mycologist. Their collaboration represents the first fusion of mycological expertise with naval engineering in dry rot prevention.
people.notes: Best known historically as inventor of the unsinkable boat (1785). His dry rot work is less well known.

people.name: Robert Seppings
people.relevance: Key figure in post-war naval reform. Accompanied Sowerby to inspect Deptford and Woolwich dockyards (Oct 1813). Sowerby praised his 'penetration & extensive practical knowledge' and 'very judicious & instructive queries.' Signed Navy Board letters commissioning Sowerby's work. Lambert's account credits Seppings (with Byam Martin) for the postwar programme to banish dry rot by improving timber seasoning, design, and strategic reserves. His diagonal bracing system strengthened ship construction and improved ventilation between frames.

people.name: F.J. Hartwell
people.relevance: Signed multiple Navy Board letters commissioning Sowerby's work (1812-1814). One of the commissioners whose 'penetration and anxiety' and 'questions' convinced Sowerby of the Board's seriousness about the dry rot problem.

people.name: H. Legge
people.relevance: Co-signed Navy Board letters commissioning Sowerby (1812-1814). Present throughout the correspondence as one of the three primary commissioners directing the dry rot investigation.

people.name: T.B. Thompson
people.relevance: Co-signed Navy Board letters including the original commission (17 July 1812) and the final payment letter (5 Jan 1814). Signed the letter awarding Sowerby 200 guineas while carefully noting the Navy Board did 'not attach to you the merit of having saved the Queen Charlotte.'

people.name: Sir William Rule
people.relevance: Sent with Knowles to Plymouth to oversee implementation of Sowerby's recommendations on the Queen Charlotte. Sowerby expressed deep gratitude that the Admiralty sent Rule and Knowles, noting 'in their experienced hands I have the satisfaction to learn it has been successful in a case rendered more desperate by the mistakes that had preceded my report.' Co-signed Navy Board letters.

people.name: Sir Thomas Byam Martin
people.relevance: Central figure in postwar timber policy. Lambert credits him with the programme to banish dry rot: building up timber reserves, improving seasoning, better design. 'Every contract or proposal had to pass through his office.' His strategy of creating a strategic seasoned timber reserve for the next major war was technically successful but came too late — design progress rendered ships obsolete before they needed repair.

people.name: Thomas Wallace
people.role: Vice-President of the Board of Trade (1818-1823); later 1st Baron Wallace
people.relevance: Chaired the 1821 Commons Select Committee on timber trade. Moderate Tory free trader, key figure in the Huskisson-era trade liberalisation. Steered the inquiry toward reducing (but not eliminating) protective duties on Baltic timber. The committee's balanced recommendation — reduce duty differential by 20s. per load — reflects Wallace's position. Connects the dry rot debate to the broader political movement toward free trade that would culminate in Corn Law repeal (1846).

people.name: Alexander Copland
people.relevance: Key witness in both 1820 Lords and 1821 Commons timber inquiries. Testified that American timber was 'very liable to dry rot' and not used 'by any professional man under government.' Put American pine joists in his own house — dry rot within 18 months. Baltic fir in same house, same conditions — no dry rot. American deals stacked in timber yard developed dry rot within 12 months; Norway deals in same conditions showed no symptoms after 8-10 years. Represents the London building trade consensus against Canadian timber.

people.name: John White
people.role: Timber merchant, London (17 years on own account)
people.relevance: Witness in both 1820 and 1821 timber inquiries. Reported red pine in a warehouse against a green wall — 'fungus had grown out of it' within 2 years. Yellow pine in his own building in Bryanstone Square — dry rot in 18 months. Identified cause as 'a stagnant atmosphere and moisture.' Noted he had 'scarcely ever known a ship built of Norway timber to have the dry rot.' Claimed initial infection came from shipping conditions — timber shipped wet in American summer. Saw two cargoes of pitch pine 'affected to the heart with dry rot as they were discharged from the ship.'

people.name: Bartin Haigh
people.role: Joiner, house carpenter and builder, Liverpool
people.relevance: Key CONTRADICTING witness in 1821 inquiry. Built 8 houses with American pine timber in 1810; examined joists four months before testimony — 'perfectly sound and good' after 10+ years. Built another house with Riga timber where joists under wine cellar were 'completely decayed' and 'all over fungus' after 9 years. Declared American yellow pine 'not more subject to decay than the common pine of the Baltic.' Represents the Lancashire experience that contradicted the London consensus.

people.name: David Bellhouse
people.role: Builder and timber merchant, Manchester (33 years experience)
people.relevance: Key CONTRADICTING witness in 1821 inquiry. Never seen American pine decay; HAS seen dry rot in Baltic timber (Dantzig and Memel). Provided vivid description of Serpula lacrymans rhizomorphs: 'strings of this fungus, like little bits of leather, running all along in the joints' of a wall — fungus spreading through masonry to infect timber in an adjacent new dining room within 12 months. This is the most precise lay description of Serpula behaviour in the entire archive.

people.name: Brodie Augustus M'Ghie
people.relevance: Witness in 1821 inquiry. Contradicted the anti-Canadian consensus: 'I never in my life saw a Quebec ship rotten in six years.' Listed 5 English-oak ships with dry rot at 6-7 years (Minerva, Batavia, Alexander, Atlas, HMS Blake). Declared dry rot 'clearly a disease' that communicates from one piece to another. Attributed primary cause to 'being cut down in an improper season, the sap being up the tree.'

people.name: Thomas Denman
people.relevance: Author of the earliest known book on dry rot (1795), previously misattributed to publisher Joseph Johnson. A physician, not a builder — treats dry rot as a medical 'distemper'. His miasmatic theory (rotted earth with 'perverted principle of vegetation') represents the pre-mycological understanding. Describes fungi spreading over brick walls to attack wood — observing Serpula behaviour without recognising the organism. His house at Sunbury Common was extensively affected. Documents epidemic-scale dry rot in 1790s London (Kensington Palace, Grosvenor Square, whole streets north of Oxford Road).

people.name: Richard Pering
people.role: Principal officer, His Majesty's Yard at Plymouth-Dock (nearly 30 years' service)
people.relevance: Author of the earliest book on dry rot in ships (January 1812). Senior dockyard officer writing from direct experience. Distinguishes fungi from dry rot as separate phenomena — 'dry-rot does not require one-half of the moisture.' Describes dry rot as contagious. Names HMS Bulwark, Foudroyant, Ocean, Queen Charlotte as premature decay cases. Estimates naval dry rot costs £20M every 8 years. Cites NO scientific literature. Published 6 months before Sowerby's inspection of the same ship (Queen Charlotte) he names as notorious.

people.name: A. Bowden
people.role: Author of treatise on dry rot
people.relevance: Won the Society of Arts gold medal for dry rot in 1818 — 35 years after the prize was first offered (1783). Cited by Knowles (1821). The long-awaited answer to the Society's call for discovering 'the cause of the Dry Rot in Timber.'

people.name: John Burridge
people.role: Merchant, tanner, author of 'Naval Dry Rot' and 'The Tanner's Key' (1824)
people.relevance: Author who explicitly linked the naval dry rot crisis to the leather trade's demand for oak bark. Argued spring/summer felling (for bark stripping) gave the navy green sappy timber, causing dry rot. Petitioned Parliament via Henry Brougham (June 1824) for a naval inquiry. Previously published a separate treatise 'Naval Dry Rot' (date unknown). Conducted research at Portsmouth dockyard. Claimed to trace the origin of dry rot to the abandonment of winter felling 'subsequently to 1792.' Provides fleet comparison data (1793/1814/1820) showing only 71 of 1,040 wartime vessels survived six years. Names specific rotten ships. Admiralty refused to engage with his proposals.

people.name: Joseph Gregson
people.role: Interior Surveyor; author of dry rot essay (1816)
people.relevance: Bridges the building and naval strands. An Interior Surveyor who surveyed HMS Stag at Deptford (Dec 30, 1811) in response to the Queen Charlotte crisis, with Admiralty and Navy Board permission. Trained in Davy's chemistry, Banks's natural philosophy, J.E. Smith's botany. Applied atmospheric/miasmatic theory to naval dry rot — pre-mycological despite writing four years after Sowerby identified the fungi. His report predates Sowerby's commission by seven months. Contains earliest known reference to Bowden's dry rot treatise (1816, before the 1818 gold medal).

people.name: Captain Markham
people.relevance: Spoke in Commons debate on naval supply, 15 March 1804. Declared merchant-built ships 'get what is called the dry rot' and 'had been the ruin of the navy.' Earliest parliamentary use of 'dry rot' for ships found in this research. Connected dry rot to crew health ('inclined the men to fevers'). Spoken during St Vincent's First Lordship.
people.notes: Likely John Markham (1761-1827), naval officer and MP for Portsmouth. Later a Lord of the Admiralty. Would need Wikidata disambiguation.

people.name: John Jervis, 1st Earl of St Vincent
people.relevance: Lambert identifies St Vincent as the direct cause of the post-1805 dry rot explosion. During the Peace of Amiens, he allowed timber stocks to run down while attacking corruption and the timber 'combination.' When war resumed, successors were forced into worse contracts and permitted use of GREEN (unseasoned) TIMBER in merchant-built battleships. 'The use of green timber was the direct cause of the post-1805 explosion of dry rot.' His zealotry about corruption inadvertently created the naval dry rot crisis.

people.name: Edmund Burke
people.role: Anglo-Irish statesman, MP (Wendover 1765-74, Bristol 1774-80, Malton 1780-94), political philosopher; sitting MP when 'dry rot' first appears in HoC Journals (1774) and when he composed the 1791 metaphor.
people.dry_rot_connection: Uses 'dry rot' as a political metaphor for internal Jacobin subversion in Thoughts on French Affairs (written December 1791, published posthumously 1797). Earliest known FIGURATIVE use of the term in elite political prose. Confirms that by 1791 'dry rot' was sufficiently familiar to a metropolitan reading public to do sophisticated rhetorical work without explanation. The specific valence — internal corruption behind an intact-looking surface — corresponds precisely to the distinctive behaviour of Serpula lacrymans (rhizomorphic spread through framing behind sound-looking finishes), suggesting the metaphor relied on lay knowledge of the organism's actual epidemiology.

parliamentary_career.significance: Burke was a sitting member of the House of Commons throughout the period when the term 'dry rot' first migrated from cordage (Gray 1725) to timber in parliamentary records (HoC Journals 1774, church floors). He was in the Commons when the Society of Arts gold medal was first offered (1783) and re-advertised through the 1780s. His 1791 metaphor is therefore not the reach of a private writer for a vaguely familiar phrase — it is a sitting MP using a term that he had likely heard, read, or seen debated in his own chamber for at least seventeen years.

sources.title: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London
sources.scope: 8,128 articles, 204 years, the world's first scientific journal
sources.ocr_status: 8,121 articles successfully OCR'd via OLMoCR on Nibi cluster H100 GPUs (7 failed on complex tables); SQLite database with FTS, page-boundary JSON, and Pagefind full-text search index
sources.role_in_project: PRIMARY CORPUS for this knowledge graph. All 25 sources below with jstor-* identifiers are individual articles from this journal. The Phil Trans is the documentary backbone of the dry rot research: full-text searched, year-stratified, and used to test the 1823 Britannica claim that dry rot was 'known at some period of Sir John Pringle's presidency.' Crucial NEGATIVE FINDING: dry rot as such is virtually absent from Phil Trans across the entire 1665-1869 run despite the 1810s naval crisis — the Royal Society did not publish on the disease. The crisis literature appeared in standalone treatises and parliamentary inquiries, not in Phil Trans.
sources.dry_rot_connection: The corpus that frames the entire project. Articles cited in this KG are linked via PUBLISHED_IN edges to this node.

sources.title: The Royal Navy suitably manned, being the safety of Great Britain
sources.key_quote: Also a Method to preserve the Rigging of the Royal Navy, the Cordage from Dry-Rot and the Sails from Mildew; the Two great Enemies in length of Time &c. And Timber may be so Managed as to endure as long again.
key_claims: EARLIEST KNOWN USE of the term 'Dry-Rot' found in this research — 1725
Applied to CORDAGE (rope), NOT timber — describes rope fibres becoming brittle and powdery
Paired with 'Mildew' as 'the Two great Enemies' of naval stores
Timber mentioned separately — 'Timber may be so Managed as to endure as long again' — but dry rot is NOT applied to timber
Naval context — rigging, sails, cordage
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL FOR ETYMOLOGY. The term 'dry-rot' existed by 1725 for CORDAGE decay — 53 years before Mahon applied it to timber (1778). The term later migrated from rope/cordage to timber when a similar decay pattern appeared in wood. This migration of terminology likely coincided with the spread of Serpula lacrymans. The 1823 Britannica claim that the disease was 'not known by its present name beyond the middle of the last century' probably refers to the TIMBER application, not the pre-existing term for cordage.

sources.title: The City and Country Purchaser, and Builder's Dictionary: or, The Compleat Builders Guide (2nd edition)
sources.key_quote: Alder: ...if it lie always wet, it will harden like a very stone, but where it is sometimes wet, sometimes dry, it rots immediately.
key_claims: NO entry for 'dry rot' in an alphabetical builder's dictionary
Same Alder passage as Scamozzi/Leyburn 1708 — common source or direct borrowing
Rot understood purely as moisture problem — alternating wet/dry causes decay
Published the SAME YEAR as Gray 1725 uses 'Dry-Rot' for cordage — the building trade had no equivalent term for timber
sources.dry_rot_connection: NEGATIVE EVIDENCE. An alphabetical builder's dictionary (1726) has no entry for 'dry rot.' The building trade had no concept of dry rot in timber even though the Navy was using the term for cordage in the same year (Gray 1725). The term had not yet migrated from naval stores to construction.

sources.title: The Mirror of Architecture: or the Ground-Rules of the Art of Building (5th edition, with Leyburn's Compendium)
sources.key_quote: Timber ought not (especially Oak) to be wrought very wet, nor too dry; for too wet makes it more apt to rot, and too dry, more hard to work.
key_claims: Only concern about timber is simple moisture management: too wet rots, too dry is hard to work
NO mention of 'dry rot' as a named condition
NO awareness of paradoxical decay in dry timber
NO mention of fungi, spreading decay, infectious disease of timber
NO special precautions about plaster, enclosed spaces, ventilation in relation to rot
Fifth edition of a widely-used manual — reflects standard builder knowledge of the time
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL NEGATIVE EVIDENCE. A standard, widely-used building manual in its 5th edition (1708) shows NO awareness of dry rot. Builders understood that wet timber rots — the ancient, simple relationship. Compare to Mahon 1778, just 70 years later, warning about 'the dry-rot' as a known hazard of enclosed damp plaster. Something new entered British building experience between 1708 and 1778.

sources.title: Memoires Relating to the State of the Royal Navy of England, For Ten Years, Determin'd December 1688

key_passages.p18_powder: their Neighbouring Timbers in many places perish'd to powder, to the rendring them unable with safety to admit of being breem'd, for fear of taking Fire; and their whole sides more disguis'd by Shot-boards nail'd, and Plaisters of Canvas pitch'd thereon (for hiding their Defects)
key_passages.p87_toadstools: Their Holds not clean'd nor air'd, but (for want of Gratings and opening their Hatches and Scuttles) suffer'd to heat and moulder, till I have with my own Hands gather'd Toad-stools growing in the most considerable of them, as big as my Fists
key_passages.p86_neglect: the plain Omission of the necessary and ordinary Cautions us'd for the preserving of New-built Ships
key_passages.p93_infection: how deeply the Ships were infected with that evil, by which they were (even in their Thickest stuff) become rotten and reduc'd to powder. It seems a matter of too great presumption (without wholly stripping them) to undertake against any possible remains or returns of the said evil

key_claims: Pepys gathered fungal fruiting bodies (toadstools) from ship holds with his own hands
Toadstools grew in unventilated, uncleared holds — wet rot conditions
Timber 'perish'd to powder' in multiple ships, some disguised with boards and canvas
Commissioners describe decay as 'infection' with potential for 'remains or returns'
30 new ships (Parliamentary programme) deteriorated to near-worthlessness through neglect
Cause attributed entirely to omission of maintenance — no biological agent suspected
Investigation rejected three popular causes: hasty building, green timber, East-Country (Baltic) timber
Conference of Master Builders (April 17, 1686) endorsed foreign plank as equal or superior
English timber supply already insufficient — 100 loads foreign for every 20 English
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL BASELINE. The 1680s naval timber crisis was wet rot caused by neglect. Pepys — FRS, PRS — shows no surprise at the TYPE of decay, only its EXTENT. No novel organism is suspected. This establishes that dry rot (Serpula lacrymans) had NOT yet arrived, or was not yet distinguishable from ordinary wet rot. The 140-year gap between Pepys's 'perish'd to powder' (1684) and the 1823 Britannica's 'reduces it to a mass of dry dust' marks the period of Serpula's arrival and recognition.

sources.title: An Account of Mr. Sutton's Invention and Method of Changing the Air in the Hold, and other close Parts of a Ship
key_claims: Confined air loses elasticity and becomes unhealthy
Proposed using galley fire to draw foul air from ship holds
Focus entirely on crew health, not timber
sources.dry_rot_connection: Addresses the environmental conditions (damp, confined, unventilated holds) that favour Serpula lacrymans, but without any awareness of biological agency

sources.title: Some Observations upon Mr. Sutton's Invention to extract the foul and stinking Air from the Well and other Parts of Ships
key_claims: Air in ships vitiated by bulge-water putrefaction AND overcrowding
Links atmospheric corruption to both disease and material damage
Acid nitrous gas in cool air prevents putrefaction
sources.dry_rot_connection: Watson's framework treats putrefaction as a unified chemical process affecting flesh and materials alike — the intellectual basis for later anti-rot treatments

sources.title: A Method of preventing Ships from Leaking, Whose Bottoms are eaten by the Worms
key_claims: Ship bottoms eaten by marine worms (Teredo)
Proposed filling void between planks with boiling pitch
Pitch fills every cranny, makes ship 'tight as a Bottle'
sources.dry_rot_connection: Biological destruction of ship timber — but marine worms (Teredo), not fungi. Shows the worm problem was the dominant concern for ship hulls pre-1750

sources.title: An Account of a Book
key_claims: Phosphorescence ('Phosphori') of 'dry rotten Wood is weak, and not lasting'
Most remarkable in 'rotten Wood of the Fir-tree'
Observing bioluminescence from wood-decay fungi WITHOUT recognising it as biological
sources.dry_rot_connection: Phrase 'dry rotten Wood' is descriptive, not a disease name. But they are literally observing the metabolic activity of wood-rot fungi. Fir (a softwood) is noted as particularly susceptible — consistent with Serpula lacrymans behaviour

sources.title: Further Experiments on Substances Resisting Putrefaction
key_claims: Tested various antiseptic substances against putrefaction of organic matter
Salt of hartshorn, nitre, chamomile as antiseptics
Whatever preserves flesh 'will be generally antiseptic'
sources.dry_rot_connection: Established Pringle as THE authority on putrefaction — explains the 1823 Britannica claim. His framework dominated how all organic decay was understood for decades

sources.title: A Continuation of the Experiments on Substances Resisting Putrefaction
A Letter concerning the green Mould on Fire-Wood
key_claims: Microscopic study of green mould (fungus) on firewood
Observed 'Numbers of minute Fungus's' with fruiting bodies and spores
Describes fungal morphology under magnification
British scientists CAN observe fungi on wood microscopically
sources.dry_rot_connection: Demonstrates that the tools existed to identify fungi on wood, but no one made the causal connection between fungi and timber decay. The conceptual framework (putrefaction theory) precluded biological explanations

sources.title: A Treatise on Ventilators
sources.key_quote: Where the Earth under the Ground-floors of Houses is damp, and close confined, it not only rots in a few Years the Joice and Boards of the Floors, but also, by its ascending putrid Vapour, the Very Wainscot of those Rooms, as it does the upper Works of Ships: Whereas, when the Earth under Ground-floors is dry, they do not rot, notwithstanding the Air is close confined under those Floors
key_claims: FIRST explicit connection of ship timber rot to building timber rot
Describes rot ascending through buildings: damp earth → floor joists → wainscoting
Links to ships: 'as it does the upper Works of Ships'
Control observation: dry ground = no rot even in confined air — moisture is the initiating factor
Still attributed to 'ascending putrid Vapour' — miasmatic framework, no biological agent
No term 'dry rot' — just 'rots'
Expanded version of 1755 Phil Trans paper
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL BRIDGE SOURCE. Hales in 1758 sees the same rot in buildings AND ships — 20 years before Mahon names 'dry-rot.' The behaviour he describes (rot ascending from damp ground through floors to wainscoting) is consistent with Serpula lacrymans, but also with native wet rot. The control observation (dry ground = no rot) shows the rot still requires moisture to initiate. Hales has no name for this beyond 'rot' and no concept of fungal agency — he blames 'putrid Vapour.'

sources.title: An Account of the Great Benefit of Ventilators in Many Instances, in Preserving the Health and Lives of People, in Slave and Other Transport Ships
key_claims: Ship timber decay in harbour is a serious, ongoing problem
Decay 'wholly owing to damp, close, confined, putrid, corroding air'
Ventilation proposed as remedy for BOTH crew health AND timber preservation
References Captain Thomson of the Success Frigate
sources.key_quote: the preserving much longer from decay the timbers of ships laid up in ordinary in harbour. For, as we are assured by daily experience, that the decay is wholly owing to damp, close, confined, putrid, corroding air
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL PASSAGE. First explicit recognition in Phil Trans that ship timber is decaying in harbour — precisely the conditions where Serpula lacrymans thrives. The miasmatic explanation (bad air) is wrong, but the observation of the problem is correct. Could mark the early phase of Serpula spread in British dockyards

sources.title: Some Experiments on Putrefaction
key_claims: Chemical theory of putrefaction — volatile alkali (ammonia) and phlogiston
Acknowledges Pringle's and Bacon's foundations
Published during Pringle's presidency

sources.title: On the noxious Quality of the Effluvia of putrid Marshes
key_claims: Addressed directly to Pringle
References Pringle's 'excellent treatise on the diseases of the army'
Demonstrates putrid air is noxious to living organisms
Putrid matter 'will preserve other substances from putrefaction' (saturation theory)

sources.title: The Method taken for preserving the Health of the Crew of His Majesty's Ship the Resolution
key_claims: Addressed to Pringle as PRS
Methods: sweet-wort, sour krout, portable soup, rob of lemons
Keeping ship 'clean and dry between decks'
Aired with fires, or 'smoaked with gunpowder moistened with vinegar'
Sugar as antiscorbutic, oil suspected as harmful
sources.dry_rot_connection: Cook's obsessive drying and ventilation of the ship parallels exactly what would prevent Serpula lacrymans — but his concern is crew health, not timber. Placed within Pringle's antiseptic framework

sources.title: Description of a most effectual Method of securing Buildings against Fire
key_claims: FIRST USE of 'dry-rot' as named condition in Phil Trans
Context is BUILDINGS not ships
Used casually without definition — term already known
Prevention: ensure materials are 'most perfectly dry' before enclosing
Paper primarily about fireproofing with plaster and lath
sources.key_quote: very particular attention must be paid to the rough plaster-work and to the sand being most perfectly dry before the boards are laid, for fear of the dry-rot
sources.dry_rot_connection: The earliest use of the specific term in the Phil Trans. That Mahon uses it without explanation confirms it was already in common circulation by 1778. The building context (damp plaster + enclosed timber = dry rot) perfectly describes Serpula lacrymans conditions

sources.title: An improved Method of tanning Leather
key_claims: Communicated BY Pringle to the Royal Society
Tanning preserves leather 'from rotting' using oak-bark astringents
Detailed chemistry of anti-putrefaction treatment
Directly relevant to timber preservation chemistry (tannins)

sources.title: Account of a Petrefaction Found on the Coast of East Lothian
key_claims: Addressed to Pringle as PRS
Iron solutions harden and preserve stone
Proposes iron washes to protect Portland stone from 'injuries of the weather'
References Dr. Fothergill's observations
Framework of preventing material decay through chemical treatment

sources.title: Some Account of the Termites, Which are Found in Africa and Other Hot Climates
key_claims: Termites as 'the greatest plagues of both Indies'
Wooden structures hollowed out while appearing sound externally
Biological agent destroying wood — but tropical insects, not fungi

sources.title: Concerning the difference of Timber growing in different Countries, and felled at different Seasons of the Year
key_claims: Unexpected timber rot attributed to 'inward decay in the Tree before it was felled'
Trees 'begin to decay at first in the midst or Heart of the Tree'
Riga/Danzig oak 'Spongy and brittle' vs English/French oak
Timber quality varies by origin and growth rate
sources.dry_rot_connection: Pre-Serpula framework for understanding unexpected rot — attributed to the living tree, not to post-harvest fungal colonisation. This explanation would be inadequate for dry rot's rapid destruction of sound timber

sources.title: Observations on the Grafting of Trees
key_claims: Canker disease in fruit trees — bark discoloured, wood dead to centre
Wood of old varieties 'soft and spongy under the knife'
Attempted to cure by repeated grafting — disease persisted
Fungal pathology in trees described without mycological vocabulary

sources.title: An account of some experiments made with the fire-plates, together with a description of the manner of application, and an estimate of the expence
sources.key_quote: With respect to the accident which sometimes happens to the timbers of an House, and which is commonly called by the term Dry-Rot, and which we now impute to the want of a circulation of air; we do not know the real cause of it. Nothing is more common than to find the Dry-Rot, as it is called, in wainscots, which certainly cannot be from want of air. I conceive the most material cause of the Dry-Rot, or rather what should be called the Wet-Rot, to be from adventitious damp steaming upon the timbers, as upon Ground-floors, where there may chance to be some spring or damp soil underneath.
key_claims: Term 'Dry-Rot' is COMMONLY USED by 1776 — 'commonly called by the term'
Cause is UNKNOWN — 'we do not know the real cause of it'
Current theory (lack of air) is INSUFFICIENT — wainscots in open rooms get dry rot too
Hartley CHALLENGES THE NAME — 'or rather what should be called the Wet-Rot'
Proposes damp as the real initiating cause — 'adventitious damp steaming upon the timbers'
The wainscot puzzle: rot appearing in open rooms contradicts the ventilation theory — consistent with Serpula spreading beyond the damp zone
This is the Hartley that Mann cites in 1778 — the fireproofing inventor
sources.dry_rot_connection: MOST ANALYTICALLY IMPORTANT SOURCE. Hartley in 1776 confirms the term was 'commonly called,' admits the cause was unknown, notes the ventilation theory fails to explain wainscot rot, and proposes renaming it 'Wet-Rot.' The wainscot puzzle — rot in open rooms — is consistent with Serpula lacrymans spreading via rhizomorphs beyond the damp zone. Hartley is observing the organism's distinctive behaviour without understanding it.

sources.title: Journals of the House of Commons (Anno 14 Georgii III Regis, 1774)
sources.publication: Journals of the House of Commons, session Nov 1772 - Sept 1774. Printed by order of the House of Commons, [1775?]
sources.key_quote: That the Walls have imbibed the Damp, by which a dry Rot has destroyed the Floors and Pews, and likewise the Timbers which support the Building, by which the Whole is become dangerous; and it is absolutely necessary the same should be re-built.
key_claims: EARLIEST CONFIRMED USE of 'dry Rot' applied to TIMBER found in this research — 1774
In Parliamentary records — official government language, not specialist vocabulary
Describes a church: floors, pews, structural timbers destroyed, requiring complete rebuilding
Mechanism described: walls imbibe damp → dry rot destroys timber throughout building
Textbook Serpula lacrymans pattern: moisture in masonry enabling fungal attack on structural timber
During Pringle's presidency (1772-1778) — confirms the 1823 Britannica claim exactly
Predates Mahon 1778 by four years
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL SOURCE. Earliest confirmed use of 'dry rot' for TIMBER in Parliamentary Papers — confirmed by systematic search of the digitised parliamentary record (ProQuest Parliamentary Papers). The 1804 Markham speech is the earliest use for SHIPS. CAVEAT: OCR quality limits certainty — earlier mentions may exist in records that are not searchable with current OCR. The mechanism described (damp walls → dry rot in timber throughout a building) is consistent with Serpula lacrymans behaviour. The term is used without explanation, suggesting it was already understood by non-specialists.
sources.search_method: ProQuest Parliamentary Papers full-text search confirmed 1774 as earliest hit for 'dry rot' in parliamentary records. Earlier records may contain unsearchable mentions due to OCR limitations.

sources.title: Memoire
sources.publication: Observations sur la Physique, sur l'Histoire Naturelle et sur les Arts, vol. XII, pp. 249+
key_claims: Introduces the English term 'Dry-rot' to a French scientific audience
Glosses it as 'ce que les anglois nomment Dry-rot, ou Pourriture sèche'
Context is building materials — moisture in planks and risk of rot
Mann was FRS and connected to Banks; published in Phil Trans 1779
Confirms the term was established in England and circulating internationally by 1778
sources.dry_rot_connection: Contemporaneous with Mahon (July 1778). The phrase 'what the English call' confirms dry rot was named in England first and was being exported to Continental scientific discourse. Mann's connection to Banks and the Royal Society shows the international network sharing this knowledge.

sources.title: Transactions of the Society, instituted at London, for the Encouragement of Arts, Manufactures, and Commerce, vol. 3
sources.key_quote: Preventing the Dry Rot in Timber. To the person who shall discover to the Society, the cause of the Dry Rot in Timber, and disclose a certain method of prevention, superior to any hitherto known; the gold medal. The accounts of the causes and methods of prevention, confirmed by repeated experiments to be produced to the Society on or before the second Tuesday in December, 1785
key_claims: Society of Arts offers its GOLD MEDAL — highest award — for discovering the cause and prevention of dry rot
Cause still UNKNOWN in 1783 — 'discover to the Society the cause'
Existing prevention methods inadequate — 'superior to any hitherto known'
Deadline December 1785 — same month as Keate v. Adams trial where Banks testified
Society of Arts was housed in the ADELPHI — the Adam brothers' building that later suffered dry rot itself (c.1800, treated by Dr. Higgins)
The 1772 Premiums list has NO dry rot prize — prizes for planting timber (acorns, chestnuts, elm, fir) but no timber preservation or decay
The Society went from NO AWARENESS (1772) to GOLD MEDAL URGENCY (1783) in about 11 years
Annual Premiums lists (1758-1790+) available on Internet Archive — full series should be OCR'd to pinpoint when dry rot first appears as a category

olmocr_analysis.method: Complete Premiums offered (1759-1780) and Abstract of Premiums (1784-1789) series processed through OLMoCR

results.1759-1780: NO dry rot prize in any volume — 20+ years of premiums without mention
results.1781-1782: MISSING VOLUMES — critical gap, not yet located
results.1789: Item 156 — UPGRADED to 'gold medal, or silver medal and ten guineas'

olmocr_analysis.conclusion: Prize re-advertised annually with escalating incentives through at least 1789. According to Knowles (1821), A. Bowden eventually won the gold medal for a treatise on dry rot in 1818 — 35 years after it was first offered.
olmocr_analysis.significance: The Society went from NO AWARENESS (1759-1780) to GOLD MEDAL URGENCY (1783-1789). The 1772 Premiums list has prizes for PLANTING timber but nothing on timber preservation or decay. This transition to urgency occurred in the early 1780s, consistent with Serpula lacrymans becoming a crisis in London buildings.

sources.dry_rot_connection: Shows dry rot was a major institutional concern by 1783. The gold medal prize confirms the cause was unknown and existing remedies insufficient. The Adelphi location connects to the Adam brothers thread: they built the Adelphi (1768-1772), were sued for dry rot in another building (1785), and their own Adelphi building got dry rot (c.1800).

sources.title: Exchequer: KEATE against Mell. ADAMS (dry rot lawsuit)
key_claims: George Keate sued the Adam brothers (architects of the Adelphi) for dry rot in a new building
Dry rot attributed to Linnets's cement trapping moisture on walls
Phrase 'the common AND dry rot' distinguishes two types of rot as legally separate categories
Sir William Chambers and Mr. Paine arbitrated — found no cause of action
Follow-up report: dry rot 'has become so prevalent in parts of this Metropolis'
Sir Joseph Banks, President of the Royal Society, testified as expert witness on wood decay
Banks testified on dry rot in court but no Phil Trans articles on dry rot appeared during his presidency (1778-1820)
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL SOURCE. Earliest newspaper use of 'dry rot' found in this research (1785). Distinguishes 'common' and 'dry' rot as separate legal categories. Shows dry rot appearing in NEW construction. Banks's testimony connects the PRS directly to the dry rot problem — yet it never entered the Phil Trans.

sources.title: Some Observations on that Distemper in Timber called the Dry Rot
sources.publication: London: Printed for J. Johnson, No. 72, St. Paul's Church-Yard, 1795
sources.authorship_correction: Previously attributed to publisher Joseph Johnson. Title page reads 'By Dr. Denman M.D.' Manuscript annotations identify Thomas Denman (1733-1815), prominent obstetrician and physician. J. Johnson was the printer/publisher.
key_claims: EARLIEST KNOWN BOOK-LENGTH TREATMENT of dry rot (1795)
Author is a physician, not a builder or naturalist — treats dry rot as a medical-style 'distemper'

Cites LEVITICUS 14 ('the plague or leprosy in the house') as a precedent — same passage Knowles (1821) would cite. CAVEAT: the Leviticus passage (greenish/reddish streaks in walls) is NOT dry rot — likely saltpetre efflorescence, rising damp staining, or mould. Both Denman and Knowles retrospectively appropriate it to legitimise their subject.
Theory: rot originates from 'distempered ground' with 'a perverted principle of vegetation' — geological/miasmatic, not biological
Fungi observed ('the fungous web which ever accompanies the rot') but not clearly identified as cause vs symptom

Describes fungus spreading over brick walls: 'the fungous may often be seen mantling over its surface, yet without hurting it. But if it chance to meet any wood in its passage, there it fixes and seems to acquire fresh vigour'
Rot 'ASCENDS from the part where it was formed, and does not descend or spread much laterally' — later reader annotates 'This is a mistake, it both descends & spreads laterally'
Footnote: Society of Arts had offered premiums for dry rot prevention — Denman learned this only as pamphlet was going to press
Named London locations affected: Grosvenor Square area, Piccadilly (Milnes house), Hanover Square, Kensington Palace, St. James's Park
Manuscript annotation: 'Whole Streets in the new Buildings, on the other side Oxford Road, it is said, have the Dry Rot' — epidemic scale
References Professor Grassman of Stettin's timber treatment with mineral coal and turf ley
Denman's own house at Sunbury Common (built c.1740s, neglected from c.1770) was extensively rotten
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL SOURCE as the earliest book on dry rot. Written by a physician who treats the problem medically ('distemper', 'infection', 'cancer in wood'). His miasmatic theory (rotted earth → perverted vegetation → timber decay) represents the pre-mycological understanding. His observation that fungus 'mantles over' brick walls but only attacks wood it encounters describes Serpula lacrymans behaviour without recognising it as the causal agent. The epidemic scale he documents (Kensington Palace, whole streets north of Oxford Road, houses near Grosvenor Square) confirms the building dry rot crisis was already severe by 1795 — contemporary with Sowerby's Tab. CXIII.

sources.title: Essay on the Causes of the Dry Rot in Buildings; contained in a series of letters
sources.publication: London: J. Taylor, Architectural Library, 59 High Holborn, 1803
key_claims: Author: John Papworth, Architect (later John Buonarotti Papworth)
'The various attempts of a century to cure the dry rot having proved unsuccessful' (p.42) — pushes awareness back to ~1700
FUNGUS EXPLICITLY IDENTIFIED AS CAUSAL AGENT: 'parasitical, like the misletoe, eventually destroys the parent stock'
Five classes of dry rot: from earth, from walls, from timber itself, and two other causes
Cites Spallanzani on mould generation, Pliny on fungi origins, Buxbaum on fungal propagation by root, Leone Battista Alberti (15th C) on washing sand to prevent fungi in mortar
WARNS air can SPREAD the disease: 'too often, instead of injuring the fungus, it has considerably assisted its vegetation'
Compound disease theory: 'every attempt to eradicate the disease must be futile, unless the productive properties of each [cause] are separately counteracted'
No mention of Adelphi, Adam brothers, Society of Arts, Navy, or ships — entirely a buildings treatise
Fungus description: 'in the earth it is fibrous and perfectly white, ramifying in every direction in the form of roots' — describes rhizomorphs

ocr_analysis_april_2026.structure: Six letters addressed to G.E.J. Wright Esq. Letter I: preliminary observations on fungal origins. Letter II: appearances of the fungus. Letter III: causes (5 classes). Letter IV: fungus produced in timber. Letter V: other causes (putrescent vapour, intestinal decay). Letter VI: cure and air admission.

serpula_behaviour_without_name.through_brickwork: p.165: 'the branches will insinuate themselves through walls of very considerable thickness, and communicate the disease to the opposite side. On opening the bricks of walls which have appeared perfectly sound, the vegetable has been discovered passing through them in fibrous roots' — DIAGNOSTIC Serpula lacrymans behaviour (rhizomorph penetration of masonry), described 9 years before Sowerby's naval inspection.
serpula_behaviour_without_name.morphology_by_humidity: pp.159-162: In damp: 'fibrous, moderate thickness, fleshy, contains considerable quantity of water... surface pursed, various colours; centre dusky brown mixed with green, graduating into red, degenerating into yellow, terminating in white.' In drier: 'very fibrous, covered with light brown membrane... projecting from timber in white spongy excrescence, on surfaces of which a profuse humidity is frequently observed.' Colour gradient (brown-red-yellow-white) and water droplets match Serpula precisely.
serpula_behaviour_without_name.parasitical_spreading: pp.164-167: 'each ramification, no longer dependant on the stem for sustenance, takes fresh hold' — independent spreading. 'timber of moderate length will rapidly proceed to decay throughout the whole body, even though the ends only are occupied by the fungus' — Serpula spreading from wall contact points through beams.
serpula_behaviour_without_name.concealed_damage: pp.174-175: 'the superficies remains a mere shell, disguising the internal rottenness' — matches Blackburn's later description of German oak.

ocr_analysis_april_2026.five_classes_of_cause: p.190: (1) fungus from earth beneath building, (2) fungus from brickwork mortar (contaminated road sand), (3) fungus from unseasoned timber sap, (4) fungus from putrescent vapour/adjacent decay, (5) intestinal fermentative decay without visible fungus.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.road_sand_as_vector: pp.224-227: Builders used road sand containing dung and organic matter in mortar. 'When we estimate the quantity of loamy earth and dung which is collected with the sand, and the innumerable fungusses ascribable to one horseball.' Alberti (15th C) had warned against unwashed sand. Papworth's experiment: contaminated sand produced fungus; washed sand did not.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.air_double_edged: pp.350-358: Too little air encourages growth; too much spreads spores to uninfected areas. 'the application of it to the vegetable rot is similar in its effects to that of air when applied to fire, which will either extinguish or invigorate its powers.' Many buildings destroyed by ill-considered ventilation attempts.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.propagation_experiments: pp.209-216: Tested fungal propagation by seed (spores) vs root (mycelium). Clay with feculent matter produced fungus; clean clay did not. Earth from decaying trees containing microscopic roots produced fungus; earth without roots did not. — Earliest experimental work on dry rot propagation before Sowerby.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.no_species_names: Papworth never uses Boletus lachrymans or any Linnaean binomial. Vocabulary entirely generic: 'the fungus,' 'the vegetable,' 'the dry rot.' Describes Serpula's behaviour with precision but without mycological vocabulary.

sources.dry_rot_connection: THE MOST IMPORTANT PRE-SOWERBY ACCOUNT of dry rot biology. Papworth is an architect who conducted actual experiments on fungal propagation — something nobody else in the literature did before Sowerby. His description of fungus penetrating through brick walls, spreading independently from contact points, varying in form with humidity, and producing water droplets is textbook Serpula lacrymans — described in 1803 without knowing its Linnaean name. His experimental work (contaminated sand vs clean sand, earth with fungal roots vs without) anticipates Sowerby's ecological approach. His warning that ventilation can spread spores foreshadows Pering's contagion observation. His five-class causal framework is more sophisticated than most later treatments. Confirms Serpula was causing massive building damage by 1803 — the building crisis was well advanced before anyone thought to look at ships.

sources.title: A philosophical inquiry on the cause, with directions to cure, the dry rot in buildings
sources.publication: London: J. Taylor, Architectural Library, High Holborn, 1807
key_claims: Author: James Randall, Architect, of Upper Charlton Street, Fitzroy Square
