CRITICAL SUBSTRATE OBSERVATION (p.96): 'Fir wood is more often attacked by this vegetation than oak, to the latter of which it is seldom so destructive. Except when contiguous to the former, it never exhibits a fibrous appearance, but a grey kind of mould' — Serpula preferentially attacks softwood; attacks oak ONLY when adjacent to infected fir. On its own, oak gets only grey mould (different organism).
OXYDATION AS CURE: Experiments with charring and nitric acid treatment. Oxidated wood placed among active fungi remained unaffected after 60 days while all untreated wood decomposed.
Fir plank experiment (pp.104-107): fir planks thrown together in damp place 'in a few weeks utterly destroyed, leaving nothing but dust'
SOCIETY OF ARTS BUILDING ATTACKED (pp.141-142): Dr. Higgins treated with caustic ammonia and 'completely succeeded in effecting a perfect cure' — the Adelphi building itself got dry rot
ADELPHI BASEMENT (pp.146-149): stables in basement 'supplied by the dung and exuviae' — horse dung provided organic matter for fungal growth spreading to superstructure
BANK OF ENGLAND DOME (p.210): 'the great dome of the Bank of England, as originally built by Sir Robert Taylor, was destroyed by this Rot' — sound oak, attacked in the dome
PANTHÉON PARIS (p.210): fungus observed protruding from cracks near the base of the dome — international evidence
Lock Hospital chapel: fungus with 'leaf resembling that of the vine' — morphological description consistent with Serpula mycelial fans

Mr. Batson's house (1783-1793): detailed 10-year case study. Yellow fungi, white mould spreading 'like a plant, a vine, or sea weed', brick-dust coloured powder. Cured with anchor-smith's ashes (iron oxide) + charring.
Gas chemistry: fermentation produces carbonic acid and hydrogen gas depleting oxygen; fungus grows in anaerobic conditions; atmospheric air destroys fungus by burning off hydrogen
Sulphate of iron (green vitriol) as anti-fungal: 'every plant within its influence that extended several feet in the area of a circle was so affected that it withered away'
Cites Spallanzani, Priestley, Darwin, Pliny, Alberti, Buxbaum, Neckar, Humboldt, Count Rumford, Dr. Bancroft, Dr. Franklin, Linnean Society
No species names — vocabulary generic ('the fungus,' 'the plant,' 'the Dry-rot')

ocr_analysis_april_2026.fir_vs_oak: The most precise pre-Sowerby statement of substrate preference. Fir attacked by the spreading fibrous fungus (Serpula); oak attacked only when adjacent to fir, and then only by 'a grey kind of mould' (different organism). This independently confirms the substrate specificity that Sowerby would later document on ships: Serpula on softwood, different organisms on oak.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.adelphi_dryrot: The Adam brothers' Adelphi building — headquarters of the Society of Arts which was offering gold medals for curing dry rot — was itself attacked by dry rot. Horse dung in the basement stables provided organic substrate. Dr. Higgins (a chemist) cured it with caustic ammonia. This is darkly ironic: the institution offering prizes for a cure had to cure its own building.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.bank_of_england: The dome of the Bank of England, built of 'good sound oak,' was destroyed by dry rot while no other part suffered. Demonstrates that even sound oak in enclosed, unventilated conditions (a dome) could be attacked — contradicting any simple 'only softwood is vulnerable' model.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.batson_case_study: The most detailed single case study in the pre-Sowerby literature: a house in London, 1783-1793, with repeated dry rot episodes. Documents yellow fungi, white mould, brick-dust powder, and the progression from wainscot to closet to general infection. The successful cure with anchor-smith's ashes (iron oxide preventing oxygen from reaching fungal germs) is an early chemical antiseptic treatment.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.chemical_sophistication: Randall is the most scientifically ambitious of the building authors. He uses Lavoisier's new chemistry (oxygen, hydrogen, carbonic acid), cites Count Rumford on heat propagation, and proposes oxydation as a universal cure. His experiments with nitric acid and sulphate of iron anticipate modern timber treatment chemistry.

sources.dry_rot_connection: The most chemically sophisticated pre-Sowerby dry rot treatise. Randall's substrate observation (fir attacked preferentially, oak only when adjacent to fir) independently confirms the substrate specificity that Sowerby would document on ships. His experiments with oxydation, acid treatment, and iron sulphate are the earliest systematic chemical approaches to timber preservation. The Adelphi and Bank of England cases show the disease attacking prominent London buildings in the 1790s-1800s. No species names, but his morphological descriptions ('white film of mould,' 'vine-leaf' shape, 'brick-dust powder') are consistent with Serpula. Published same year as the Architectural Library (J. Taylor, High Holborn) — the same publisher as Papworth 1803, indicating a network of concerned architects sharing knowledge through the building trade.

sources.title: A Botanical Arrangement of All the Vegetables Naturally Growing in Great Britain, 2nd edition, vol. 3
key_claims: Earliest inclusion of B. lachrymans in a British botanical reference
Lists 'dry-rot' as the English common name
Claims authority for the determination: '[WITHER.]'
Description: 'Tubes white. Pileus or cap coloured, wrinkled, reticulated, with a broad, white, cottony edge'
Absent from 1st edition (1776) — added between 1776 and 1792
Sowerby (1797) later criticised Withering for having 'never saw the plant'

absence_from_1776.significance: The 1776 1st edition has comprehensive fungi coverage: ~50 Agaricus, ~13 Boletus, plus 6 other genera. Includes multiple species on rotten wood, in cellars, on buildings. Withering was NOT weak on fungi — he simply did not encounter or recognise B. lachrymans. This is strong negative evidence for the presence of Serpula lacrymans in Britain before 1776.
wood_fungi_in_1776: Comb Agaric (Pectinatus) — 'On rotten wood, in cellars, &c.'
Winding Clubtop — 'between the bark and wood of trees'
Touchwood Spunk (Igniarius) — on trunks of trees

sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL SOURCE. First British botanical identification of the dry rot fungus. Its absence from the comprehensive 1776 edition and presence in the 1792 edition brackets the arrival/recognition of Serpula lacrymans in Britain to 1776-1792. Withering likely worked from Continental sources (Wulfen 1781, Jacquin) rather than personal observation, but knew the organism was present in England.

sources.title: Coloured Figures of English Fungi or Mushrooms

tab_113.references: Jacq. Misc. Austr. v. 2. t. 8; Dickf. fasc. i. p. 18
tab_113.description: Much too common in England, taking possession even of the bond timber in houses, and often attached by the back under stair-cases. Fructification yellow to orange or bright red brown, forming circles 1-8 inches diameter, surrounded with pithy or cottony substance of pale brown. Upper part clothed with white mucor.
tab_113.etymology_english: This pithy substance, without fructification, is often found by itself, and is very dry; whence the English name of dry rot
tab_113.etymology_latin: the fructification is seldom without drops of water resembling tears, the Latin name lachrymans, or weeping, has been given
tab_113.prevention: The best way to guard against this evil, is to introduce a free circulation of fresh air, and avoid building in damp situations
tab_113.habitat: bond timber in houses, under stair-cases (buildings)
tab_113.notes: First British mycological illustration and description of Serpula lacrymans. Description matches modern identification perfectly: orange-brown fruiting body, white cottony mycelium, pale brown pithy margin, water droplets. Sowerby provides both the etymology of the English common name and the Latin binomial.

tab_289.description: Has many characters in common with B. lachrymans and B. Medulla-panis. Found growing horizontally under rotten floors, spreading in large patches, forming broad ramifications, often inosculating, of a cottony substance — 'commonly known by the name of Dry-rot'

tab_403.description: Found at bottom of a rotten bin in a cellar at Charlton House, Kent. Described as having 'strong resemblance to Boletus lachrymans, or Common Dry-rot, tab. 113, in some states'

key_claims: B. lachrymans (= Serpula lacrymans) illustrated and named as 'Common Dry-rot' at Tab. 113
Multiple related fungi described as 'commonly known by the name of Dry-rot'
Descriptions match Serpula: 'spreading in large patches', 'broad ramifications', 'cottony substance'
All specimens from buildings (under floors, in cellars), not ships
British mycologists had identified, classified, and named the dry rot organism by 1797
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL SOURCE. Sowerby provides the first British mycological illustrations and descriptions linking specific fungi to the common name 'Dry-rot'. Tab. 113 (B. lachrymans = 'Common Dry-rot') is the earliest known British scientific illustration of Serpula lacrymans. By 1797, the organism was classified, named, and connected to its vernacular identity.

sources.title: On the Causes and Cure of the Dry Rot (two letters: Sunbury and Dublin)
key_claims: Dry rot is a well-known disease of buildings by 1797
Sunbury correspondent debates whether damp is the primary cause
Discusses 'the two frequent causes of the generation of dry-rot'
Seeks remedies — the disease is well enough known to generate public debate
Dublin letter discusses dry rot in the Little House of Lords
Context is buildings, not ships
sources.dry_rot_connection: Bridges the gap between Mahon's casual 1778 mention and the post-1800 crisis literature. By 1797, dry rot was sufficiently well-known to be discussed in a general-interest periodical. The debate about causes (damp vs. other factors) shows the disease was recognized but not yet understood.

sources.title: Description of a ship at Woolwich c.1798 (cited in Britton 1875 from European Magazine, Dec 1811)
sources.key_quote: about 1798, there was, at Woolwich, a ship in so bad a state that the deck sunk with a man's weight, and the orange and brown coloured fungi were hanging, in the shape of inverted cones, from deck to deck.
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL DESCRIPTION. 'Orange and brown coloured fungi hanging in inverted cones from deck to deck' is textbook Serpula lacrymans fruiting body morphology. This is the first description we've found that sounds specifically like Serpula on a SHIP (not a building). Date c.1798 fits the timeline. CAVEAT: Known only through Britton 1875 citing European Magazine 1811 — secondary/tertiary source. The original European Magazine article should be located and verified.
sources.source_caveat: Known through Britton 1875, p.24-25. Not yet verified against original European Magazine.

sources.title: Sir Joseph Banks's account of fungi filling a wine cellar (cited in Britton 1875)
sources.key_quote: A circumstance recorded by Sir Joseph Banks is still more extraordinary, of a cask of wine which, having been confined for three years in a cellar, was, at the termination of that period, found to have leaked from the cask, and vegetated in the form of immense fungi, which had filled the cellar, and borne upwards the empty wine cask.
sources.dry_rot_connection: Another Banks/fungi connection. Massive fungal growth in a cellar, powerful enough to lift a wine cask. Demonstrates Banks's direct experience with building fungi. Original source not yet identified — Britton does not give a citation. Could be from Banks's papers, a Phil Trans communication, or oral tradition.
sources.source_caveat: Known only through Britton 1875, p.43-44. Original Banks source not identified.

sources.title: Historical timeline of timber preservation methods (extracted from Britton 1875)

key_datapoints.event: Navy authorities directed boiling and drying of treenails
Emerson patented saturating timber with boiled oil and poisons — first wood-preserving patent
Reid proposed arresting decay with vegetable acid (pyroligneous acid)
Hales recommended soaking planks at water-line in linseed oil
Jackson (London chemist) prepared timber with salt solutions for national yards — frigates 'rendered more perishable'
Practice of saturating ships with common salt prevailed. 'Vessels whose interiors were lined with fungi' cured by sinking in sea. Shows FUNGI VISIBLE IN SHIP INTERIORS during Pringle's presidency.
Hartley fireproofing experiments — George III visited experimental house on Putney Heath
Pallas (Russia) proposed steeping wood in sulphate of iron (green vitriol)
Mundic (marcasite) used 'to eradicate present and to prevent the future growth of dry rot' — early use of term
Hales proposed creosoting treenails of ships

Society of Arts building in the Adelphi attacked by dry rot — Dr. Higgins treated with caustic ammonia 'to prevent the growth of fungi'. Note: the Adelphi was the Adam brothers' building — connects to 1785 Keate v. Adams lawsuit.
Maconochie proposed saturating inferior woods with resinous/oily matters
Lukin erected kiln at Woolwich dockyard for timber preservation — fatal explosion killed 6 workers

two_organism_distinction.buildings: Merulius lachrymans (often called 'the Dry Rot') — Britton's primary dry rot organism
two_organism_distinction.ships: Polyporus hybridus ('the dry rot of our oak-built vessels') — a DIFFERENT fungus for ships
two_organism_distinction.significance: Britton distinguishes between the building fungus and the ship fungus. This may explain why buildings and ships appear as separate threads in the early evidence.

ship_decay_data.claim: three ships of 74 guns each, decayed in five years; three of 74 guns each, decayed in seven years; one of 100 guns, decayed in six years
ships of war are useless in five or six years; average duration eight years; cost of hull of three-decker nearly £100,000
has seen fungi growing so strong betwixt the timbers in a man-of-war, as to force a plank from the ship's side half an inch

sources.anachronism_warning: CRITICAL CAVEAT: Britton retroactively applies 'dry rot' to Pepys's 1680s observations. Pepys never used the term. The 1680s decay was wet rot from neglect in damp conditions. Britton's chapter title 'On the Gradual Rise and Development of Dry Rot' frames the entire history as a single continuous disease, when the evidence suggests the 1680s crisis (native brown rot) and the 1780s+ crisis (Serpula lacrymans) involved different organisms. Britton's retrospective framing should not be accepted uncritically.
sources.notes: Britton's retrospective account (1875). Treat dates and attributions with caution — secondary source written 80-100 years after events. Contains genuine primary evidence nuggets (European Magazine 1811, Banks anecdote, Chapman/Pering data) embedded in an anachronistic framing. The underlying primary sources should be located and verified independently.

sources.title: A Brief Enquiry Into The Causes Of Premature Decay, In Our Wooden Bulwarks: With An Examination Of The Means, Best Calculated To Prolong Their Duration
key_claims: Author: 'of His Majesty's Yard at Plymouth-Dock', nearly 30 years' service — senior dockyard officer
Published January 1812 — same year Sowerby inspects Queen Charlotte (July 1812)
Names HMS Bulwark, Foudroyant, Ocean, Queen Charlotte as premature decay cases (p.11)
CRITICAL DISTINCTION: 'fungus and dry-rot are not one and the same thing; for the latter does not require one-half of the moisture to keep it in a state of activity'
Dry rot is CONTAGIOUS: 'by taking a small portion of dry-rot and scattering it in a confined spot, between the wood work, the timbers, instantly, contract the complaint'
Fungi forced 'a plank from the ship's side half an inch' — physical force of fungal growth
A three-decker 'only a twelvemonth old, in a state of entire decay'
Ships 'planked over when her frame was as green as grass' — unseasoned timber
Average ship existence 'does not exceed eight or nine years'
Cost: £20 million every 8 years to replace navy; hull of a three-decker nearly £100,000
3,000 loads of oak per 74-gun ship = 2,000 large trees = 50 acres of timber cleared
HMS Royal Sovereign (1789) and Argonaut: experimental housing/ventilation failed
Royal William 'enjoyed a longer duration, perhaps, than any ship in his Majesty's navy'
Forefathers used Spanish chestnut in ancient buildings — 'will last for a thousand years'
NO scientific citations — no Sowerby, no Banks, no naturalists. Pure dockyard experience.

ocr_analysis_april_2026.fungi_vs_dryrot_distinction: Pering explicitly distinguishes two phenomena (pp.126-129): 'fungus and dry-rot are not one and the same thing; for the latter does not require one-half of the moisture to keep it in a state of activity, that fungus does.' Fungi need sustained moisture; dry rot spreads in drier conditions. This is a pre-mycological observation of the wet rot / Serpula distinction by a dockyard officer with no scientific training.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.contagion_description: 'by taking a small portion of dry-rot and scattering it in a confined spot, between the wood work, the timbers, instantly, contract the complaint, and get into as bad a state as the building or ship, from whence the rot was taken' — describes Serpula's spore/mycelium propagation as observed by a layperson.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.american_oak: p.69: 'the existence of this sort of American oak never exceeds four or five years, owing to the operation of the dry rot: consequently whatever wood comes in contact with it, must immediately take the infection'
ocr_analysis_april_2026.pitch_pine_in_piles: p.158: timber in uncovered piles has 'interiors kept in a state of partial moisture, for the formation of fungi and dry rot'
ocr_analysis_april_2026.green_timber: p.153: 'The writer has seen a ship planked over, when her frame was as green as grass' — confirms unseasoned timber was routine
ocr_analysis_april_2026.boiled_planks: pp.132-133: planks boiled in water absorb moisture; salt water boiling makes them worse. 'this kind of damp, above all others, which fosters the fungus most'
ocr_analysis_april_2026.between_decks_mechanism: pp.132-137: moisture enters through shrunk caulking, proceeds downward between timbers where no air circulates. Fungi and dry rot form in this zone.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.no_species_names: Pering never names Boletus lachrymans, Xylostroma, or any specific species. His vocabulary is generic: 'fungus', 'dry-rot', 'vegetation'. But his ecological distinction between the two phenomena is independently valid.

sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL SOURCE as the dockyard officer's perspective, written the same month Sowerby would be commissioned. Pering's distinction between fungi (needing more moisture) and dry rot (needing less, spreading contagiously) is an independent, pre-mycological observation of the wet rot / Serpula distinction — made at Plymouth dockyard in 1812 without knowledge of Sowerby's mycological vocabulary. His description of dry rot spreading from 'a small portion' to infect surrounding timber matches Serpula's rhizomorph propagation. Combined with NAUTICUS 1811 and Knowles 1821, this provides three independent witnesses to the multi-organism reality: a pseudonymous dockyard observer, a Plymouth dockyard officer, and the Navy Office secretary.

sources.title: On the Causes of the Decay of the Timber in Ships, and the Means of preventing it, and the Dry Rot

key_passages.woolwich_fireships: such a one as I recollect to have seen at Woolwich about thirteen years since, which was in so bad a state, that the decks sunk with a man's weight, and the orange and brown coloured fungi were hanging in the shape of inverted cones from deck to deck.
key_passages.species_identification: The dry rot, as it is usually called, proceeds from the growth of a parasitical plant, named by botanists boletus lachrymans, which belongs to the class of cryptogamia.
key_passages.pitch_pine: Pitch pine has been used by all nations in the construction of ships, and appears to be very superior to every other species for general durability: but this wood is the soonest destroyed by fungi, as these plants are nourished by the great quantity of resin contained in its numerous cells. I have lately seen some pitch pine plank of 7 inches in thickness completely decomposed; and, when cut open, the boletus was found to be vegetating in every part of it, but principally in the cells which were originally filled with resin.
key_passages.pepys_reference: the evil has in some measure existed; and, although it has not since appeared in so great an extent as it then did, yet the state of some ships recently launched both justifies and demands all possible inquiry

key_claims: ORIGINAL SOURCE for the 'orange and brown inverted cones' description — later quoted by Knowles (1821 p.93 without colour) and Britton (1875 accurately). Independent eyewitness account from c.1798 Woolwich.
The 'orange and brown' colour and 'inverted cone' shape are diagnostic of Serpula lacrymans fruiting bodies. This is the strongest morphological evidence for Serpula on a warship.
Names Boletus lachrymans by Linnaean binomial — but applies it generically to all ship fungi. Does not distinguish species.
Independently confirms pitch pine as most vulnerable substrate — fungi grow in the resin cells
The fungi description is from the between-decks zone (deck to deck) — the pine/deal region
Pseudonymous author ('NAUTICUS') — likely a dockyard officer, writes from practical experience
Dated 11th November 1811 — predates both Pering (Jan 1812) and Sowerby's commission (July 1812)
sources.dry_rot_connection: The single most important independent eyewitness account of Serpula lacrymans fruiting bodies on a warship. The 'orange and brown coloured fungi hanging in the shape of inverted cones from deck to deck' matches Sowerby's 1797 description of B. lachrymans exactly ('colour varies from yellow to orange, or a bright red brown... hanging in inverted cones'). This is NOT circular with Sowerby — NAUTICUS is describing what he saw at Woolwich c.1798, 14 years before Sowerby's QC inspection. The between-decks location (deck to deck) and the pitch-pine substrate confirm the spatial model: Serpula in the damp pine zone between decks.

ocr_analysis_april_2026.full_text: dryrot_sources/olmocr_results/knowles_1821_british_navy.md (310K, fully OCR'd, all 9 chapters read April 2026)
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch1_timber_properties: American oaks ranked: live oak (quercus virens) 'nearly incorruptible' — 507 pieces in USS Essex, 6 defective after 12 years. White oak (quercus alba) from Canada: 'rapid decay, and injury to that of better quality with which it came in contact.' Yellow spots = 'sure forerunners of early decay.' Red oak (quercus rubra): 'still more inferior, few instances where either kind have remained sound for more than five years.' European oaks: Provence/Adriatic best; Dantzic plank 'wears out two sets of frames.' English oak: Sussex/Kent best. Shipwrights' Company (1605/1612) founded specifically to combat poor timber in navy.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch2_felling_seasons: Winter felling debated since antiquity. Experiments show winter timber slightly heavier/stronger but 'if felled at any time and worked into a building before juices are evaporated by seasoning, and free circulation of air then prevented, it will ferment, corrupt, and be subject to early decay.' Canada oak felled in winter still rots. Key Pepys/Plott connection: Plott proposed stripping bark, Pepys supported it, James II ordered experiment at Bushy Park (never carried out due to abdication).
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch3_seasoning: Detailed experimental data: timber weights at Deptford and Woolwich 1817-1819, fresh vs salt water immersion. Immersion reduces strength by 1/7th. Proper stacking with skids, reversal of tiers, protection from weather. 'In dock-yards where timber was properly stacked, ships constructed therein have been uniformly more lasting.'
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch4_chemical_preservation: Comprehensive catalogue of failed methods: Jackson's salt/lime/copperas process (1767-1773, 9 ships of line treated, no benefit); White's lime seasoning (Amethyst 1798, timbers 'rent' and more defective than untreated); charring (Dauntless 1808, fungi grew on charred timbers); train oil (partial success — oil-penetrated parts of beams sound, rest decayed); Lukin's seasoning house (1812, oleaginous impregnation). Mineral tar experiments ongoing as of 1821. Every method tried and failed or had worse side effects.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch5_ship_construction: Seppings's diagonal trussed frame system. Construction quality affects durability: uneasy ships loosen fastenings. Solid filling between frames with cement and bricks. Doubling for strength and stability.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch6_building_precautions: Building under cover abroad vs open in England. Boiling planks in water for bending — absorbs moisture. Salt water boiling worse than fresh. Caulking before seasoning traps moisture. 'Thus the very thing which is intended to prevent fungus and dry rot, unavoidably creates them!' (matches Pering 1812).
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch7_preservation_after_building: Ventilation history: Sir John Murray 1665 (metal pipes, never published in Phil Trans), Sutton 1748, Hales 1756 (ventilators generally introduced), White's extractors 1794 (large bellows, discontinued 1815), Lukin 1808 (stoves). Ships in ordinary practice detailed. Woolwich fire-ships footnote (p.93): 'fire decks literally filled with fungi, hanging from upper to lower decks in shape of inverted cones.' QC repairs completed 1812, 'no appearance of fungus' in 1820.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.inscription: Personal copy inscribed 'To Reverend Thos. Rackett, With the Author's sincere regards' — Rackett is the man who introduced Knowles to Sowerby (B95 correspondence)
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch8_term_misapplication: p.110: 'The term dry-rot... is a late introduction... it has become a favourite name, and been given indiscriminately to almost every description of rot, whether fungus was or was not present. By the terms being thus confounded, the cause of the decay of ships cannot be accurately ascertained from official reports, but only by actual inspection.' — Knowles himself confirms laypeople applied 'dry rot' indiscriminately.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch8_fungi_ecology: p.115: 'While the Xylostroma giganteum, a fungus which resembles chamois leather, is generally confined to the rotten wood which produced it, the Boletus lachrymans spreads with a wonderful rapidity, and by taking root on the timber, or insinuating itself into the fissures, like a parasite, soon destroys the parent by which it was produced and is nourished.' — Independent ecological distinction: Xylostroma stays put, B. lachrymans spreads aggressively. This is Serpula's rhizomorph behaviour described from naval observation.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch8_qc_timber: p.117: QC built with 'great quantities of Canada oak, and American pitch-pine timber... woods, particularly the former, subject to be soon decomposed.' — ANSWERS the 'American timber' question from Lukin's letter.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch8_dryrot_above_orlop: p.122: 'from this circumstance is the dry-rot, which was found only above the orlop-deck, to be attributed' — The dry rot on QC was in the between-decks pine zone, NOT in the saturated hold. Stove heating caused condensation on cooler timbers above.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch8_sowerby_credit: p.116: 'It was at the recommendation of the author [Knowles], that that eminent botanist, Mr. Sowerby... was employed; and it is from his, Mr. Lukin's, and the dock-yard officers' reports, as well as a personal inspection, that the account is given.' — Knowles's QC account draws on Sowerby + Lukin + dock-yard officers + his own inspection.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch8_fungi_list: p.118: Lists Sowerby's fungi WITHOUT the 'very little' qualifier for B. lachrymans — presents all five species equally.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch8_woolwich_fireships: p.93 footnote: 'the fire decks were literally filled with fungi, which were hanging from the upper to the lower decks in the shape of inverted cones' — Same event as NAUTICUS 1811 but without the 'orange and brown' colour detail. Knowles's source is Navy Office records; NAUTICUS's source is personal memory.
ocr_analysis_april_2026.ch8_boletus_medulla_panis: p.125: 'some of the fungi, which have been found on the timber in ships, will grow in the open air... the Boletus medulla panis, for instance' — Knowles has independent knowledge of individual fungal species' ecology.

sources.dry_rot_connection: THE MOST AUTHORITATIVE NAVAL ACCOUNT of the dry rot crisis. Knowles was a participant (recommended Sowerby, oversaw implementation) and historian (access to Navy records from 1661). His dating of 'dry-rot' entering official naval language only in 1808 is a key data point — the DISEASE existed before the TERM was applied to ships. His Chapter VIII provides critical independent confirmation: (1) Xylostroma stays on rotten wood while B. lachrymans spreads aggressively — the ecological distinction matches Sowerby; (2) QC dry rot was 'found only above the orlop-deck' — the pine/deal between-decks zone; (3) QC was built with Canada oak and American pitch pine; (4) 'dry-rot' was applied indiscriminately to all rot regardless of fungi. Combined with his B95 letters (naming B. lachrymans on English oak ships), Knowles is the key witness bridging Sowerby's mycological expertise with the Navy Board's institutional knowledge.

sources.editorial_history: FIRST dedicated DRY-ROT article in Encyclopedia Britannica history (none in editions 1-5, 1771-1815). The text was reprinted nearly verbatim in 1842 (86% word overlap) and 1860 (97% overlap with 1842). The Chapman-based account was NEVER revised, even as scientific understanding advanced around it. In the 1842 and 1860 editions the article was swallowed by adjacent entries due to OCR/parsing failures: absorbed into DRYDEN (1842) and DRYANDER (1860).
sources.dry_rot_connection: The Britannica's first-ever dedicated dry rot article appears in the immediate wake of the Royal Navy crisis. Crisis-driven addition to existing knowledge structure (TREE, SHIP-BUILDING, FUNGI, PUTREFACTION all carried forward unchanged in 1823). Source: GraphRAG analysis of all 8 Britannica editions, 1771-1860, by jic823 / 1815EncyclopediaBritannicaNLS project (April 2026).

sources.title: Natural History (Encyclopedia Britannica, 2nd edition, 1778; reprinted 1797)
sources.key_quote: That trees, when they are dry or are cut down, may not remain useless to the world, and lie as it were melancholy spectacles, nature hastens on their destruction in a singular way: first the liverworts begin to strike root in them; afterwards the moisture is drawn out of them; whence putrefaction follows. Then the mushroom kinds find a fit place for nourishment on them, and corrupt them still more. The beetle called the dermestes, next makes himself a way between the bark and the wood... Lastly, the woodpeckers come, and, while they are seeking for insects, wear away the tree already corrupted; till the whole passes into earth.
key_claims: Clear ecological account of fungal decomposition of wood: liverworts → fungi → beetles → woodpeckers
Predates the Britannica's DRY-ROT article (1823) by 45 years
Knowledge of fungal wood decay existed as natural-history curiosity decades before being applied to the practical problem of timber decay
Located in NATURAL HISTORY rather than FUNGI, TREE, or any timber-related article — disconnected from practical timber management
sources.dry_rot_connection: DEMONSTRATES THE KNOWLEDGE GAP. The same encyclopedia that would publish the DRY-ROT crisis article in 1823 already contained, 45 years earlier, a precise ecological description of fungal succession on dead wood. The ecological knowledge was present; what was absent was the connection between this natural-history observation and the practical problem of timber decay in buildings and ships. This parallels the broader pattern of fragmented knowledge: FUNGI (1797, taxonomic), PUTREFACTION (1778, theoretical, animal-focused), TREE (1797, practical timber management) — none cross-referenced. Source: Topic-clustering analysis of 1.04M paragraph embeddings across all Britannica editions (April 2026).

sources.title: Timber, Ship-Building, Botany, Chemistry (Encyclopedia Britannica, 7th edition, 1842)
key_claims: EDITORIAL REORGANIZATION: TREE article retired to a 6-word cross-reference ('See PLANTING, TIMBER, and VEGETABLE PHYSIOLOGY'). Its 5,000 words of timber management knowledge dispersed
NEW: TIMBER article (23,015 words) — first systematic treatment of construction timbers organized by species, origin, and application; covers 20+ species
NEW: PLANTING expanded from 1,925 to 23,683 words (+1,130%) — absorbed TREE's cultivation content
NEW: BUILDING article (45,007 words) — 49 timber mentions
EXPANDED: SHIP-BUILDING from 67,432 to 114,684 words (+70%) — new sections on timber decay, preservation science, dry rot pits
EXPANDED: NAVY from 631 to 31,020 words (+4,800%) — from administrative summary to technical treatise
NEW PRESERVATION CHEMISTRY: Kyanizing (corrosive sublimate / mercuric chloride) first mentioned in SHIP-BUILDING and RAILWAYS
NEW PRESERVATION CHEMISTRY: Creosoting first mentioned (1 mention, in PUTREFACTION)
MERULIUS first named in BOTANY and CHEMISTRY — the first time Britannica connects a binomial fungus to dry rot

TIMBER article describes Canadian and American timbers in detail — 11 mentions of yellow Canada pine, extensive treatment of red Canada pine (sap-wood analysis); also Indian (teak: 60 mentions; sal, sissoo, jarrol) and African (Tortosa / African oak: 16 mentions) timbers
Larch account drawn from Knowles, 'On Preserving the Navy' — direct citation of Knowles 1821 by Britannica
sources.dry_rot_connection: Marks the transition from a single crisis article (1823) to a distributed body of timber/decay knowledge embedded across material science (TIMBER), engineering (SHIP-BUILDING), administration (NAVY), cultivation (PLANTING), and biology (BOTANY). Critically, the DRY-ROT article itself is REPRINTED VERBATIM and never updated — the new knowledge accretes around it. The naming of MERULIUS in BOTANY 1842 marks the first Britannica use of the modern mycological binomial for dry rot, 61 years after Wulfen's classification (1781) and 45 years after Sowerby's first British illustration (1797).

sources.title: Timber, Ship-Building, Botany (Encyclopedia Britannica, 8th edition, 1860)
key_claims: BOTANY mycelium description: dry rot is 'a Fungus which spreads its mycelium through the texture' of wood — first explicit Britannica statement of the modern pathological model. BOTANY's fungi mentions grew from 1 (1842) to 68 (1860)

Burnett's process (chloride of zinc) discussed in TIMBER alongside Kyan's and Margary's methods

lloyds_durability_table.context: First systematic comparative durability evaluation in the entire Encyclopedia Britannica; from Lloyd's Register of Shipping

ratings_years.12: Teak, live oak, greenheart, morra, iron-bark and other 'superior woods'
ratings_years.10: Mahogany (hard texture), Cuba, Sibon, pencil cedar, Adriatic/Spanish/French oak
ratings_years.9: Red cedar, Angelica, Venetian; other continental white oaks, Spanish chestnut, stringy bark, blue gum
ratings_years.8: North American white oak, American sweet chestnut

lloyds_durability_table.editorial_note: The article observed that 'a ship built of the very best species of timber, thoroughly seasoned, can be classed as a first-class ship for twelve years only' — and that this classification had an 'injurious influence,' since 'sufficient inducements are apparently not held out for increasing the durability of ships.'

sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL CORROBORATION OF KNOWLES 1821. The 1860 Britannica's explicit statement that American timber is 'much softer... more liable to the dry-rot' and 'seldom used in ship-building' confirms, from an independent encyclopedic synthesis 39 years later, the durability disaster Knowles documented for the 1810s Canadian frigates. The Lloyd's table places North American white oak at 8 years and American red pine at 5 years — consistent with Knowles's ~3-year average for Canadian softwood frigates and his observation that English oak could last a century. The BOTANY 1860 mycelium passage marks the complete arrival of the biological-pathological model: dry rot is now unambiguously a fungus spreading through wood texture, not a chemical putrefaction. Source: GraphRAG analysis of 1842 and 1860 editions, jic823 / 1815EncyclopediaBritannicaNLS project (April 2026).

sources.title: Speech on naval supply, Commons Sitting of Thursday, March 15 1804
sources.key_quote: The ships built in the King's yards, were wholesome and sound for the men to live in; those built elsewhere, were generally found to get what is called the dry rot; they inclined the men to fevers, and were soon rendered unserviceable. He might even say, that those ships which were built in merchants' yards, had been the ruin of the navy.
key_claims: EXPLICIT DISTINCTION between King's yard ships (sound) and merchant-built ships (dry rot)
Merchant-built ships 'the ruin of the navy' — strongest parliamentary language yet on dry rot
Dry rot linked to CREW HEALTH: 'inclined the men to fevers' — fungal growth associated with disease
Phrase 'what is called the dry rot' — term established in parliamentary usage by 1804

sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL DATA POINT filling the gap between the 1790s building crisis and the 1812 naval crisis. Captain Markham in 1804 identifies merchant-built ships as the source of dry rot — exactly the ships built with green timber under the contracts St Vincent's successors were forced into. The health dimension (fevers from fungal growth) connects to the older Hales/Sutton tradition of ship ventilation for crew health, but now the cause is explicitly named as dry rot rather than 'putrid air.' This is the earliest parliamentary use of 'dry rot' for SHIPS found in this research (the 1774 House of Commons use was for a church building). CAVEAT: based on OCR-searchable parliamentary record; earlier unsearchable mentions cannot be ruled out.

sources.publication: Manuscript, 34 pages. Now held at British Museum Natural History (date stamp 26 Jun 1937).

key_passages.commission_17jul1812: We desire you will proceed with Mr Lionel Lukin to Plymouth, as early as possible, & inspect the state of His Majesty's Ship Queen Charlotte, which has recieved very considerable injury by the Dry rot, with a view to ascertain what measures it may be advisable to adopt to stop its further progress if possible, by preventing the vegetation & spreading of the Fungi.
key_passages.sowerby_opening: Shocked at the report of the rotting state of many of our National Bulwarks I felt ready to make enquiry... I happened to be called upon for that purpose; & I therefore felt it an imperative duty... I know of nobody who had condescended to study sufficiently the habits of Fungi, I had no alternative.
key_passages.cause_identified: Confined Damp, is so palpable that it might be thought easy to remove; yet, from the day of King Charles to the present time of increased experience & improvement, its ravages have become more and more distressing.
key_passages.fungi_on_qc: Boletus hybridus E.F. tab. 289, being the young state of Boletus medulla-panis E.F. 326. (White rugged soft Fungi) Xylostroma giganteum (A Gigantic Leather-like Fungus peculiar to rotten Oak) E.F. 358. Auricularia pulverulenta E.F. 214 & some others of but little consequence. There were very little remains of Boletus Lachrymans E.F. 113, the best known Dryrot.
key_passages.hygrometer_data: An Hygrometer was left for an hour near the pump well in the Hold; it indicated a very damp air standing at 828.6° outside the ship it had stood at 464.2° perfect wetness being indicated by 1000°. I have found by experiment that Wood will rot & Fungi grow at 800° or 900°.
key_passages.chemical_treatments_rejected: I concieve that any impregnation of the wood is unnecessary, if the wood be properly dried; and many acid and or saline impregnations I have heard recommended as perfect cures, are certainly detrimental to the wood, bolts & workmen.
key_passages.parker_debunked: You, of course, must be somewhat aware of the delicacy of my situation & see that to give an opinion upon his ideas is as difficult as to interpret Nebuchadnezzars dream. Conscious of your previous information and judgement I venture to presume that you did not before understand the Dryrot in Ships as unanimously admitted to be caused by an Insect.
key_passages.payment_caveat: we do not attach to you the merit of having saved the Queen Charlotte by your advice, all the defective materials having been taken out of that Ship at Plymouth when she was repaired there.

key_claims: FIRST DOCUMENTED CASE of a mycologist being commissioned by the Navy Board to diagnose dry rot using scientific methods
Sowerby identified fungi on HMS Queen Charlotte by his own English Fungi plate numbers — cross-referencing his 1797 taxonomy directly to naval practice
Found VERY LITTLE B. lachrymans (the known 'dry rot' of buildings) but ABUNDANT B. hybridus and Xylostroma giganteum — supports Britton's later two-organism distinction
Used hygrometer to quantify conditions (828.6° in hold vs 464.2° outside) — earliest known quantitative measurement of ship environment in relation to fungal growth
Diagnosed cause as 'Confined Damp' and 'fermentation' — rejected insect theory and chemical treatments
Admiralty adopted his recommendations fleet-wide (28 Aug 1812) — ventilation, hygrometers, timber seasoning
Sowerby subsequently inspected Deptford and Woolwich dockyards with Seppings (Oct 1813)
Commissioned to build 6 experimental timber stacks at Deptford under cover with ventilation (Nov 1813)
Recommended seasoning timber under shelter from rain and sun, raised from ground, with free air circulation
Awarded 200 guineas (Jan 1814) — Navy Board credited his work but denied he 'saved' the Queen Charlotte
Correspondence shows the Navy Board received and rejected quack cures — Parker's insect theory, various chemical preparations
Sowerby's approach was ecological: understand fungal habits, control environment (moisture, air), rather than apply chemical treatments
References Pepys-era decay: 'from the day of King Charles to the present time... its ravages have become more and more distressing'

fungi_identified_on_qc_formal_report.name: Boletus hybridus
fungi_identified_on_qc_formal_report.notes: Young state of B. medulla-panis EF 326. 'White rugged soft Fungi.' Nomenclatural databases (GBIF, MycoDB, Index Fungorum, MycoBank) all place within synonymy of F. vaillantii — a brown-rot polypore of coniferous timber (order Polyporales). Previous KG entry of 'Donkioporia expansa (probable)' was INCORRECT — D. expansa has a separate basionym (Boletus expansus Desm. 1823). Britton (1875) calls this Polyporus hybridus — 'the dry rot of our oak-built vessels.' 1911 Encyclopædia Britannica preserves same distinction.

fungi_identified_on_qc_formal_report.name: Xylostroma giganteum
fungi_identified_on_qc_formal_report.notes: 'A Gigantic Leather-like Fungus peculiar to rotten Oak.' Sowerby ranked it as most destructive. B97: 'the most destructive Oak Leather... with a gigantic stride... rending it in all directions to a mere powder.' Also found in pitch pine timbers in the dockyard. Fine specimens in Master builders counting house at Plymouth.

fungi_identified_on_qc_formal_report.name: Boletus lachrymans
fungi_identified_on_qc_formal_report.notes: 'The best known Dryrot.' Sowerby found VERY LITTLE of this. B98 full species list confirms: 'seldom'. B98 draft explicitly distinguishes: 'Boletus lachrymans common dry rot found more in damp building on shore.' Sowerby Book: originally wrote 'but little remains', corrected to 'very little remains' — both mean barely present. B98 page 57 variant 'very little [most of] remains' was NOT adopted in the published version.

full_dockyard_species_list_b98_p25.note: Complete inventory from B98 page 25 — fungi found across timber in yards, piles, ship frames, and finished ships. ~25 species vs 4 in formal report. 'G.C.' = Queen Charlotte specifically.

species.name: plus varieties, Conferva, etc.
species.notes: Sowerby notes 'varieties in different states many... besides Conferva &c'

humidity_fungi_scale_b97_b98.note: Sowerby's humidity-fungi correlation from B97 page 78 and B98 page 45 — early quantitative mycological ecology, 1812-1813.

thresholds.effect: Less dangerous but rain must be avoided
Mucor beginning — first indicator of rot (B98 p.45: '300 - Mucor beginning')
Safe range for seasoning (Sowerby Book: 'I have found agreeable & safe')
At first sight of rot, Fungi may grow. B98 p.45: '600 - Xylostroma simplex'
Further sign of destruction. B98 p.45: '700 - intermediate ones'

Boletus Lachrymans and B. medulla panis likely to grow (B97 initially wrote 'Dry rot' then corrected to 'Boletus Lachrymans'). B98 p.45: '800 - Bol. Medulla panis and Xylostroma gigantea or Oak leather'
Xylostroma giganteum — 'Oak Leather shewing wood in a very destructive and useless decayed state'
Approaching or quite wet — every change back producing fresh fermentation damage
Above B. lachrymans threshold (700-800), in Xylostroma zone (800-900) — consistent with finding very little lachrymans but abundant Xylostroma

sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL SOURCE — THE ROSETTA STONE connecting Sowerby the mycologist (1797) to the naval dry rot crisis (1812). His finding of abundant B. hybridus but very little B. lachrymans on the Queen Charlotte is a key data point: the 'dry rot' destroying ships may not have been the same organism as the 'dry rot' destroying buildings. This supports Britton's two-organism distinction (Polyporus hybridus for ships, Merulius lachrymans for buildings) and partially answers open question q12. The hygrometer data (828.6° in hold) provides the first quantitative environmental measurement linking ship conditions to fungal growth thresholds.

b96_field_notebook: Contains NO species identifications — Sowerby identified fungi retrospectively in London from specimens/drawings, not in the field
Contains travel diary: London to Plymouth July 21-24, 1812 (route via Greenwich, Richmond, Bagshot, Basingstoke, Andover, Amesbury, Wincanton, Exeter)
Contains timber stacking sketches — visual thinking behind stacking recommendations
Contains note on tannin principle: 'might be managed at Dartmoor... make Oak of any hardness'
Contains note on Harmattan winds preventing rot — dry wind = protection
Names 'Mr Whidbey' alongside Dartmoor — possible contact for timber experiments
Emotional register: Sowerby sees himself as 'an instrument in the hands of providence' for this work
Contains early hygrometer reading variant: '850 inside, 1160 outside' (vs published 828.6/464.2)
b97_rough_notes: Contains earliest QC draft with uncertain identifications: 'perhaps Boletus hybridus' and 'M. Lachrymans Dry rot?' with question mark
Contains page 88: categorized fungi list by location — 'found in the timbers bringing to the dock, those on the piles in the dock, frames of the ships and those on the finished ships'
Contains humidity-fungi scale correlating specific species to specific hygrometer readings (300-900°)
Contains ranking of destructiveness: Xylostroma most destructive, then B. lachrymans ('more external'), then B. hybridus ('still more imperious')
Contains financial records: expenses £40.12.6, claimed 5 guineas/day for 60 days, received 200 guineas
Contains Linnean Society paper draft (Dec 5, 1815) about Lycoperdon cancellatum
Xylostroma found 'also others in the pitch pine nearly related to it' — confirms mixed timber substrates
b98_fair_copies: Contains FULL SPECIES LIST (~25 species) on page 25 — vs 4 in formal report. B. Lachrymans explicitly marked 'seldom'
Contains Sowerby's own two-organism distinction (page 16): B. hybridus + Xylostroma as ship destroyers vs B. lachrymans 'found more in damp building on shore'
Contains humidity-fungi correlation scale (page 45): Mucor at 300°, Xylostroma simplex at 600°, B. medulla panis + Xylostroma gigantea at 800°

Page 57 textual variant RESOLVED: 'very little [most of] remains of Boletus lachrymans' — the 'most of' insertion was NOT adopted in the published version. Published text settles on 'very little remains'. Two-organism interpretation holds.
Page 25 also shows crossed-out 'boletus lachrymans' at top of page — further evidence Sowerby initially considered including it more prominently but reduced its prominence
b95_correspondence: Knowles (15 Jul 1812) reveals HE initiated the commission: 'I have frequently stated both at the Admiralty and this Board the necessity of taking your opinion'
Knowles introduced Sowerby through Mr Rackett
Knowles describes Lukin's failed method: stoves placed in hold and on decks to destroy 'the Boletus' by intense heat
Admiralty overrode Lukin: 'repairs shall be proceeded with according to your suggestions without further reference to Mr Lukins reports'
HMS Vigo — new ship built ~3 years prior, heated per Lukin's plan, now in 'bad state from the dry rot'
Lukin asks (8 Oct 1812): 'was the Queen Charlotte built of American Timber?' — significant for substrate question
Legge personally approved report: 'I really approve very much of this Report, and I think it will do you very great Credit'
Sowerby snubbed in 1817: Seppings asked for free advice privately, Sowerby refused — expenses submitted twice to Knowles but unpaid
Payment receipt note: 'Rec'd the money at the bank Jan 15th 1813' (misdated? letter is Jan 1814)

sources.title: The Last Sailing Battlefleet: Maintaining Naval Mastery 1815-1850 (Chapter 3: Timber: Supply and Seapower)
key_claims: Robert Albion's Forests and Seapower (1926) fundamentally misunderstood British timber supply — the supply never failed
Oliver Rackham calculated woodland suitable for oak was 10x greater than needed for naval supply
Earl St Vincent's zealous attack on timber corruption (1801-1804) ran down dockyard stocks and forced successors into worse contracts
USE OF GREEN (UNSEASONED) TIMBER in wartime was 'the direct cause of the post-1805 explosion of dry rot'
Queen Charlotte 'somewhat unfairly considered to have been the most rotten ship ever to be built for the Navy'
Byam Martin and Seppings set postwar priority on seasoned timber reserves — 'to banish the spectre of dry rot from the Navy'
Programme was technical success but 'came too late in the history of the wooden warship' — design progress made ships obsolete before repair
North American timber 'does not possess, for ship-building, more than one half the durability of Wood of the same description the produce of the north of Europe' (1820 Lords Committee)
Canadian timber 'particularly liable to the growth of fungi, that species of vegetation which is the forerunner of dry rot' (1820 Lords Committee)
Oak timber from Memel, Dantzic, and Riga most suitable for ship-building; Canadian oak and fir vastly inferior
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL SECONDARY SOURCE for understanding the political economy of the naval dry rot crisis. Lambert demonstrates the crisis was caused by ST VINCENT'S POLICIES (running down timber stocks, forcing use of green timber) not by timber supply failure. This reframes the naval dry rot problem: the organism exploited a POLITICAL failure (unseasoned timber policy) just as it exploited an ARCHITECTURAL failure (enclosed construction) in buildings.

sources.title: First Report [Relative to the Timber Trade] from the Select Committee of the House of Lords appointed to inquire into the means of extending and securing the Foreign Trade of the Country
sources.publication: Ordered by The House of Commons to be Printed, 11 July 1820
key_claims: North American timber 'does not possess, for the purpose of ship-building, more than one half the durability of Wood of the same description the produce of the north of Europe'
Canadian timber 'particularly liable to the growth of fungi, that species of vegetation which is the forerunner of dry rot'
American timber 'has even had the effect of destroying that which was in union with it' — spreading infection to adjacent timber
Canadian timber use now 'discontinued in His Majesty's dock-yards, in consequence of the unfavourable result experienced'
System of protective duties since 1809 had forced use of inferior Canadian timber
Copland: 'many a gentleman has got the dry rot in his house' from speculator buildings using Canadian timber
White: Norwegian ships last 80-90 years; 'we never knew a Norway ship to have the dry rot'
White identifies cause: 'A stagnant atmosphere and moisture not free from air nor with a free circulation'
Seppings provides frigate durability table: Baltic fir frigates lasted ~8 years, Canadian fir frigates ~3-4 years
sources.political_context: Petition from Baltic timber merchants triggered this inquiry. The protective duties (1809-1813) had forced substitution of inferior Canadian timber for superior Baltic timber. The free trade lobby used dry rot as evidence that protectionism harmed consumers. The Marquess of Lansdowne (chair) was a leading Whig free trader. This is the FIRST of two reports — the 1821 Commons committee continued the investigation.
sources.dry_rot_connection: CRITICAL SOURCE. Parliamentary evidence confirming the timber quality dimension of the naval dry rot crisis. Canadian timber was officially inferior and 'particularly liable to the growth of fungi.' Dry rot becomes a FREE TRADE ARGUMENT: protectionist tariffs forced consumers to use fungus-prone timber, causing dry rot in ships and buildings alike. Seppings's frigate durability data (8 years Baltic vs 3-4 years Canadian) provided the quantitative foundation. The building trade witnesses (Copland, White) extended the argument from ships to houses — speculator-built London houses with Canadian timber were getting dry rot within 18 months.

sources.title: First Report from the Select Committee appointed to consider of the Means of improving and maintaining the Foreign Trade of the Country [Timber Trade]
sources.publication: Ordered by The House of Commons to be Printed, 9 March 1821
sources.chair: Thomas Wallace (Vice-President of the Board of Trade, moderate Tory free trader)
key_claims: Committee acknowledges 'much prejudice subsists' against Canadian timber but notes Lancashire experience is more favourable
Geographic split in testimony: LONDON witnesses anti-Canadian timber; LANCASHIRE witnesses pro-Canadian timber
Seppings repeats: American timber 'peculiarly subject to dry rot'; frigate durability 8 years (Baltic) vs 4 years (Canadian)
Seppings introduces CONTAGION THEORY: American timber 'inoculates' other timber — fungus starts on American oak and spreads to English timber in contact
Copland: dry rot in his own house within 18 months from American pine joists; Baltic fir in same conditions — no dry rot
White: fungus grew out of red pine in a green wall within 2 years; 'moisture is the principal cause of dry-rot'

Haigh (Liverpool): CONTRADICTS London witnesses — built 8 houses with American pine (1810), still 'perfectly sound and good' after 10+ years; Riga timber in same builder's house 'completely decayed' with fungus after 9 years
Bellhouse (Manchester): never seen American pine decay; HAS seen dry rot in Baltic timber (Dantzig and Memel); describes fungus spreading through a wall as 'strings like little bits of leather'
Wilson (Quebec): 'Have you heard of many houses being infected with the dry rot? — No, never in my life'
M'Ghie (shipbroker): 'I never in my life saw a Quebec ship rotten in six years'; lists 5 English-oak ships with dry rot at 6-7 years
Russia Company Memorial: American wood 'spread, to an enormous extent among ships and houses, the previously rare disease of the Dry Rot' — most politically charged framing
Committee recommends reducing duty differential from 3l. 5s. to 2l. 5s. per load — moderate liberalisation

appendix_data.seppings_frigate_tables: Baltic fir frigates (built 1795-1806) vs Canadian pine frigates (built 1812-1813). All timber received 1793-1813.
appendix_data.barlow_strength_tests: Peter Barlow experiments on timber samples (8ft x 2in x 2in). Red pine: sg 657, breaking weight 511. Yellow pine: sg 553, breaking weight 420. Riga fir: sg 753, breaking weight 422. Norway spar: sg 577, breaking weight 655.
appendix_data.cost_comparison: 46-gun frigate: Canada fir timber costs 7,387l; Baltic fir (after deducting duty) costs 3,717l. Duty on Baltic makes Canadian timber the same price.
appendix_data.russia_company_memorial: Formal petition arguing Canadian timber 'spread the previously rare disease of the Dry Rot' — most explicit political use of dry rot as argument against protectionism

sources.political_context: Continuation of the 1820 Lords inquiry, now in the Commons under Thomas Wallace (Tory moderate free trader). The committee received testimony from a wider range of witnesses, including northern English builders who CONTRADICTED the London consensus on Canadian timber. The geographic split (London anti-Canadian, Lancashire pro-Canadian) reflects different building practices and timber markets. The committee's balanced recommendation (reduce but don't eliminate duty differential) shows the political difficulty of acting against colonial interests even when evidence of harm existed. Wallace's role connects this to the broader Huskisson-era trade liberalisation movement.
sources.dry_rot_connection: THE MOST EXTENSIVE PRIMARY SOURCE on dry rot in the entire knowledge graph (45 mentions across 184 pages). Shows dry rot functioning as a political argument in the free trade vs protectionism debate. The GEOGRAPHIC SPLIT in testimony is analytically significant: London builders reported catastrophic failure of Canadian timber; Lancashire builders reported no problems. This may reflect different building practices (ventilation, wall construction) rather than timber quality alone — consistent with Sowerby's finding that environment (confined damp) matters more than timber species. Bellhouse's description of fungus as 'strings like little bits of leather running all along in the joints' of a wall is a vivid description of Serpula lacrymans rhizomorphs spreading through masonry — exactly the behaviour that distinguishes dry rot from wet rot.

sources.title: Thoughts on French Affairs
sources.publication: In Three Memorials on French Affairs. Written in the years 1791, 1792 and 1793. By the late Right Hon. Edmund Burke. London: printed for F. and C. Rivington; sold also by J. Hatchard, 1797
sources.key_quote: If they were to guard against an invasion from France, the merits of this plan of a merely defensive resistance might be supported by plausible topics; but as the attack does not operate against those countries externally, but by an internal corruption from dry rot, they who pursue this merely defensive plan, against a danger which the plan itself supposes to be serious, cannot possibly escape it.
key_claims: EARLIEST KNOWN METAPHORICAL/POLITICAL USE of 'dry rot' in elite English prose (composed December 1791)
Burke uses dry rot to figure Jacobin subversion as internal, hidden, and spreading behind a sound-looking exterior — distinctively the behaviour of Serpula lacrymans, not generic decay
The metaphor presupposes lay familiarity with dry rot among Burke's readership; he offers no explanation
Sits chronologically between Hartley 1776 ('commonly called') and Denman 1795 ('epidemic') in the term's diffusion timeline

Establishes that by 1791 'dry rot' was already a public-discourse term, fifteen years before the Queen Charlotte was laid down and twenty years before Knowles (1821) claims the term was popularised by the QC crisis

Posthumously published 1797 in Three Memorials on French Affairs (F. and C. Rivington, London); the metaphor was therefore in print and circulating during the years of the Society of Arts gold medal campaign and the Denman/Papworth/Randall building-crisis literature

Burke was a sitting MP (Malton, 1780-94) when he composed this in December 1791. He had been in the Commons continuously since 1765 — including 1774, when 'dry rot' first appears in House of Commons Journals as a TIMBER term. The metaphor is therefore drawn from the working vocabulary of the legislature, not borrowed from outside it.
sources.dry_rot_connection: Provides an external, non-technical, datable confirmation that the term and the phenomenon were familiar to educated metropolitan readers by 1791. The specific metaphorical valence (internal/hidden/spreading) is diagnostic for Serpula behaviour and suggests the figure was reaching for what made dry rot distinctively alarming — not just decay, but invisible structural compromise.
sources.parliamentary_significance: Closes a loop with the §V parliamentary inquiries of 1820-21: dry rot was being talked about in and around the House of Commons throughout the half-century between 1774 (first appearance in HoC Journals) and 1821 (Commons Select Committee on Timber). Burke's 1791 metaphor sits at the midpoint of that arc and demonstrates that the term had become a standing reference in parliamentary culture well before it was weaponised in the tariff debate.

sources.title: A discovery has recently been made... The Queen Charlotte, of 120 guns
sources.key_quote: The Queen Charlotte, of 120 guns, the building of which ship lasted some years, in Deptford dock-yard, has on her arrival at Plymouth, been declared by the officers of that yard, to be in a rapid state of decay, owing to the dry rot. This discovery, it seems, arose from its being found necessary to caulk the ship's top-sides where the effects of the rot appeared visible. The timber in which the decay has taken place, principally consists of Canada oak. The Queen Charlotte was launched on the 17th of July, last year, at Deptford, and went to Sheerness, where she lay several months to season; and not long since proceeded under charge of Captain Morris, and a proper number of officers and men, from that place to Plymouth.
key_claims: EARLIEST KNOWN NEWSPAPER REPORT of the Queen Charlotte dry rot crisis (September 1811)
Identifies 'Canada oak' as the timber principally affected — the newspaper blamed Canadian timber from the start
Rot discovered incidentally during caulking of top-sides, not through a deliberate inspection
Reports QC launched July 17, 1810, at Deptford; sent to Sheerness to season; then sailed to Plymouth under Captain Morris
Gun count given as 120 (cf. Knowles: 100, second Times report July 1812: 110; actual: 104)

Predicts the discovery 'will, doubtless, lead to an investigation of the timber in the dock-yards' — the public demand for inquiry that culminated in Sowerby's commission (1812) and the parliamentary inquiries (1820-21)
sources.dry_rot_connection: Contemporary public evidence for the moment the Queen Charlotte crisis broke. Confirms Knowles's 1821 claim that the term 'dry rot' was popularised by the QC's defective state being discovered in 1811. The information was in the Times from the start — the public narrative of Canadian timber and dry rot was established in September 1811, nine months before Sowerby's inspection.

sources.title: The Queen Charlotte, of 110 guns, built at Deptford
sources.key_quote: The Queen Charlotte, of 110 guns, built at Deptford, (which had been fumigated for the dry rot, and is now in dock in Plymouth-yard) is found on opening to be in such a state, that every top-timber and tree-nail is to be taken out, preparatory to a general inspection. The Queen Charlotte has never been at sea, except in the passage from Deptford to Plymouth.
key_claims: Confirms fumigation was attempted and FAILED — 'found on opening to be in such a state' after treatment
'Every top-timber and tree-nail is to be taken out' — this is the stripping that explains why Sowerby found 'very little' B. lachrymans three weeks later (July 24-25, 1812)
'Has never been at sea, except in the passage from Deptford to Plymouth' — the ship rotted without ever serving
Published July 6, 1812 — just 18 days before Sowerby's inspection on July 24
Gun count given as 110 (cf. Sept 1811 Times: 120; Knowles: 100; actual: 104)
sources.dry_rot_connection: The second Times report on the QC, nine months after the first. Documents the failure of chemical treatment (fumigation) and the decision to strip the ship completely — directly explaining the condition Sowerby encountered when he arrived. The detail that the QC 'has never been at sea' is devastating: a first-rate warship rotted in dock without ever serving.

sources.title: An Essay on those Defects of Buildings dependant on the Nature and Properties of Air: containing a Report on the Cause of the Dry Rot in the Royal Navy, and the Means of Prevention as now adopted in His Majesty's Dock Yards
sources.publication: London: printed for James Ridgway, Piccadilly; and sold by J. Taylor, Architectural Library, High Holborn
sources.dedicated_to: Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathern
sources.published_by_permission: Lords of the Admiralty and of the Navy Board
sources.epigraph: Ex aquâ oritur aër, ex aëre æther. — Cic.
key_claims: BRIDGES BUILDING AND NAVAL STRANDS: an Interior Surveyor writing about the navy with Admiralty permission
Contains 'Report of a Survey taken on Board His Majesty's Ship the Stag, building in Deptford Yard, December 30th, 1811' — survey made 'in consequence of the Queen Charlotte being infected'
Distinguishes DRY ROT from WET ROT as separate sections (pp. 15 and 22)
Identifies two causes of dry rot: (1) quality of the timber, (2) the situation in which it is placed — parallels Sowerby's later environmental emphasis
Key finding: 'the soundness of a ship may be then said to depend, NOT ON THE LENGTH OF TIME, BUT ON THE WEATHER WHILE BUILDING' (p. 18, caps in original)
Notes timbers framed together 'without the least possibility of discharging the accumulated moisture of rain, snow, or dews' (p. 18, italics in original)
Recommends awnings over ships during construction, preventing mixing of different saps, proper air circulation
Air holes at top of timber spaces 'instead of counteracting, only promotes the rot, by feeding it with the nitrogen of the atmosphere' — wrong chemistry, right instinct
Notes reforms already being carried on in Royal Dock Yards by 1816 — 'a new æra in the annals of ship building' (p. 20)
Connects to Lloyd's ratings: vessels 'well known and rated at Lloyd's according to their sea worthiness' (p. 20)
Notes green timber problem from 'prevailing custom of felling oak for the bark, when the sap is most in it' — links dry rot crisis to oak bark industry conflict
Footnote: 'At one period heart of oak only was allowed to be used, and all the sap or alburnum cut away. Hence, perhaps, the reason of the old song — Hearts of Oak are our Ships'
References Hales on ventilating ships (p. 40)
Contains 'Observations on Mr. Bowden's Treatise on the Dry Rot' (p. 57) — earliest known reference to Bowden's work, two years before Bowden won the Society of Arts gold medal (1818)

Miasmatic framework: treats dry rot as an atmospheric problem, connects timber decay to human disease (gaol distemper, contagious fevers). Pre-mycological despite writing four years after Sowerby identified the fungi

Intellectual network: attended lectures by Sir Humphrey Davy, Brande (Chemistry); Dalton (Pneumatics); Banks (Natural Philosophy); Sir J.E. Smith (Botany — president of Linnean Society). Cites Priestley, Franklin, Count Rumford, Smeaton

sources.dry_rot_connection: A buildings surveyor trained in Davy's chemistry, applying atmospheric theory to naval dry rot with Admiralty sanction. Fills the gap between Pering (1812) and Knowles (1821). His Deptford survey (Dec 1811) was prompted by the QC crisis and predates Sowerby's commission by seven months. Documents reforms already underway in the dockyards. The miasmatic framework (air, not fungi) shows that even with Sowerby's mycological work available, the dominant explanation remained atmospheric. The oak bark connection (spring vs winter felling) links the dry rot crisis to the bark/tanning industry — relevant to the user's other article.

sources.title: The Tanner's Key to a New System of Tanning Sole Leather, or the Right Use of Oak Bark, &c.
sources.publication: London: printed for Charles Frederick Cock, 64, Paternoster-Row
sources.epigraph: To save Old England's Naval Glory, United be both Whig and Tory.
sources.author_description: Author of 'Naval Dry Rot,' &c. — merchant of London, Nelson Square, Blackfriars-road
sources.previous_work: Naval Dry Rot (date unknown, pre-1824) — a separate treatise on the origin, danger, effects, and remedies of naval dry rot
key_claims: CENTRAL ARGUMENT: The leather trade's demand for oak bark forced spring/summer felling of oaks, filling the timber with sap; the navy then built with this green sappy timber, causing dry rot. The abolition of winter-hewn oak dates to 'subsequently to 1792'
'£250 worth of timber is thereby sacrificed, in order to save £25 worth of bark' (p. 97) — the economic ratio of naval loss to tanning profit
Claims to have 'positively traced the origin of the dry rot to the abolition of the use of winter hewn oak subsequently to 1792' (p. 96)
Cites statute of King James I (1605) requiring winter felling of naval oaks — abandoned for bark profits
Fleet data: 1793: 153 sail of line, 411 vessels; 1814: 251 sail, 1,040 vessels; 1820: 149 sail, 510 vessels. Only 71 of 1,040 vessels from 1814 remained by 1820 (pp. 98-99)
Named rotten ships: Genoa, Lord Nelson, Rippon, Diamond, Queen Charlotte (p. 95)
Geopolitical warning: 'America and France are now building a navy with timber hewn in winter with the bark on... I predict, Dry Rot alone will give them power to subdue our colonies' (p. 92)

Sap theory: 'winter hewn oak is much better for immediate use... nor time evaporate the sap of timber, in general, if hewn at bark harvests or seasons, when fully impregnated and abounding with sap, which is the seed of dry rot' (p. 101)

Banks connection: 'in the year 1802, the late Sir Joseph Banks, president of the Royal Society, brought to the Court's notice, that terra japonica... possessed ten times the strength of the oak bark' (p. 76)
Contains formal petition to Parliament presented by Henry Brougham, June 14, 1824, praying for naval inquiry into dry rot
Admiralty refused to engage: 'applied to the Lords' Commissioners of the Admiralty, who declined entering into the question' (p. 96)
Includes sworn affidavit (Mansion-house, May 14, 1821) that he tanned leather using oak tops/lops/coppice from bark harvest, proving bark-stripped wood usable
East India Company terra japonica import data 1810-1824 (p. 78)
Includes 'Observations on Mr. Bowden's Treatise on the Dry Rot' — references the Society of Arts gold medal winner

sources.dry_rot_connection: Burridge's argument adds a fifth contingency to the assemblage: the leather trade's demand for oak bark forced spring/summer felling, filling naval timber with sap. This is NOT the same as Lambert's seasoning argument (wartime urgency) — Burridge says the problem predates the war and traces to the bark industry's economic incentives. The £250/£25 ratio and the petition to Parliament show this was a live political issue in the 1820s, parallel to the Canadian timber tariff debate. For the dry rot article, this belongs in §III (the timber strand) as evidence that green timber was not only a wartime emergency but a structural feature of the oak bark economy. For the oak bark article, this is a primary source.

sources.title: Dry Rot
sources.original_source: Plymouth, Feb. 17 [1818] — reprinted in The Times (London), Friday Feb. 20, 1818, issue 10385
sources.notes_on_provenance: The Phil Mag text is a word-for-word copy of a Plymouth dispatch dated Feb 17, 1818, also published in The Times on Feb 20, 1818. The original is a provincial naval report; Phil Mag and the Times both reprinted it.
sources.key_quote: Not a ship is taken into dock but is found to be nearly rotten. The very best ships do not average more than twelve years existence.
key_claims: Names FIVE ships affected by dry rot: Eden (sloop), Topaze (frigate), Dartmouth (frigate, 3 years old, never at sea), San Domingo (74-gun, ripped up at 4 years at Portsmouth), Queen Charlotte (110-gun)
Eden sunk in Hamoaze to cure dry rot — failed; defective in every part on opening
Dartmouth frigate 'three years old, never at sea' — parallels QC's fate
'Not a ship is taken into dock but is found to be nearly rotten' — the crisis was fleet-wide
'The very best ships do not average more than twelve years existence' — matches Knowles's declining durability data
Lukin ('Dr. Lukin, an Admiralty chemist') received £5,000 for 'ineffectual labours to stop the progress of vegetation in the ship' — names the cost of failed chemical treatment
Uses 'vegetation' for the fungus — pre-mycological language treating fungi as plants
QC 'built at Woolwich' — contradicts Times (Deptford) and Knowles (Deptford); may reflect confusion or multiple yards involved
