sources.dry_rot_connection: A scientific periodical reporting the fleet-wide dry rot crisis with specific ship names, ages, and costs. Published 1818 — between the naval crisis (1811-14) and the parliamentary inquiries (1820-21). Shows the scientific community was registering the scale of the disaster. The £5,000 Lukin payment is new evidence. The sinking of Eden in Hamoaze as a 'cure' for dry rot is a remarkable example of pre-scientific treatment.

events.description: Encyclopedia Britannica 2nd edition NATURAL HISTORY article publishes ecological account of fungal succession on dead wood
events.significance: Same year Mahon casually uses 'dry-rot' in Phil Trans, Britannica describes the underlying ecology (liverworts → fungi → beetles → woodpeckers) — but as natural-history curiosity, not connected to timber decay. The knowledge gap, not the knowledge, was the obstacle. 45 years before Britannica's first DRY-ROT article.

events.description: Encyclopedia Britannica 6th edition publishes first dedicated DRY-ROT article (8,983 words, Chapman authority)
events.significance: First standalone dry rot entry in Britannica history — added in immediate wake of naval crisis. Same edition also expanded DOCK from 370 to 21,463 words. Article reprinted nearly verbatim in 1842 (86% overlap) and 1860 (97%) and never revised. New knowledge accreted around it rather than replacing it.

events.description: Britannica 7th edition reorganizes timber knowledge: TREE retired, TIMBER (23,015w) and BUILDING (45,007w) created, SHIP-BUILDING expanded 70%, NAVY expanded 4,800%; Merulius first named in BOTANY/CHEMISTRY
events.significance: Editorial transformation from a single crisis article (1823) to distributed timber/decay knowledge across material science, engineering, administration, cultivation, and biology. Kyanizing and creosoting introduced as preservation chemistry. First Britannica use of Merulius binomial — 61 years after Wulfen, 45 after Sowerby. Knowles 1821 directly cited in TIMBER's larch account.

events.description: Britannica 8th edition SHIP-BUILDING article includes Lloyd's Register durability classification; BOTANY describes dry rot as fungus spreading mycelium; American timber explicitly downgraded as dry-rot-prone
events.significance: First systematic quantitative durability table in Britannica: teak/greenheart/iron-bark = 12 years; American white oak = 8; American red pine = 5; hemlock = 4. Independently corroborates Knowles 1821 on Canadian/American softwood failure 39 years later. BOTANY's 'Fungus which spreads its mycelium through the texture' marks complete arrival of biological-pathological model. New Australian timbers (jarrah, iron-bark, tuart) introduced as dry-rot-resistant alternatives.

events.description: Sir John Pringle serves as President of the Royal Society
events.significance: Period when dry rot was allegedly first known to the RS according to 1823 Britannica

events.description: Franz Xaver von Wulfen classifies Serpula lacrymans
events.significance: First formal mycological classification of the dry rot fungus

events.description: First use of 'dry-rot' as named condition in Philosophical Transactions
events.significance: Term used casually, implying it was already in common use

events.description: Stephen Hales identifies ship timber decay in harbour as serious ongoing problem
events.significance: Possibly marks early institutional recognition of worsening timber decay — could correspond to early phase of Serpula spread

events.description: Keate v. Adams dry rot lawsuit in Court of Exchequer; Banks testifies as expert
events.significance: Earliest newspaper use of 'dry rot'. Distinguishes 'common and dry rot' as separate categories. Dry rot in NEW building. Joseph Banks (PRS) testifies as expert — yet dry rot never enters the Phil Trans. Shows the term and concept were established in legal and public discourse by 1785.

events.description: William Withering adds B. lachrymans ('dry-rot') to his Botanical Arrangement, 2nd edition
events.significance: First British botanical identification of the dry rot fungus. Absent from 1st edition (1776). Brackets British recognition of Serpula lacrymans to 1776-1792.

events.description: James Sowerby publishes first British mycological illustration and description of Boletus lachrymans ('Common Dry-rot')
events.significance: Connects the specific fungus to the English common name 'dry rot'. Provides etymologies for both names. Describes the organism as 'much too common in England'. Prescribes ventilation as prevention. All specimens from buildings. Completes the scientific identification of the dry rot organism in Britain — 16 years after Wulfen's Continental classification (1781).

events.description: Gentleman's Magazine publishes letters debating causes and cure of dry rot
events.significance: Earliest known discussion of dry rot in a general-interest British periodical. Disease is well-established enough for public debate about causes and remedies. Context is buildings (Sunbury, Dublin Parliament). Bridges 1778 (Mahon) and 1818 (M'William treatise).

events.description: Ship at Woolwich with 'orange and brown coloured fungi hanging in inverted cones from deck to deck'
events.significance: First description that sounds specifically like Serpula lacrymans fruiting bodies on a SHIP. Orange-brown colour and inverted cone shape match modern identification. Deck sinking with a man's weight shows advanced timber destruction. CAVEAT: known through Britton 1875 citing European Magazine 1811.
events.source_caveat: Tertiary source — verify against European Magazine Dec 1811

events.description: HMS Queen Charlotte (104-gun first-rate, launched July 17, 1810) found in rapid decay from dry rot at Plymouth, 1811
events.timber: Canada oak and American pitch pine (Knowles p.117)
events.significance: Emblematic of the naval dry rot crisis. Rot discovered incidentally during caulking (Sept 1811 Times). Fumigated — failed (July 1812 Times). Stripped of every top-timber and tree-nail before Sowerby inspected (July 24-25, 1812). Never served at sea 'except in the passage from Deptford to Plymouth' (Times July 1812). Lukin received £5,000 for ineffectual chemical treatment (Phil Mag 1818). The ship was built from the crisis, of the crisis, and never used.
timeline: 1810-11: sent to Sheerness to season, then to Plymouth under Captain Morris
Sept 1811: decay discovered during caulking at Plymouth; Times publishes report (Sept 19)
1811-12: fumigated for dry rot (Times July 1812); Lukin's chemical treatment, cost £5,000 (Phil Mag 1818)
July 6, 1812: Times reports fumigation failed, total strip-down ordered
Aug 28, 1812: Admiralty adopts Sowerby's recommendations fleet-wide

events.description: Explosion of dry rot treatises and publications — at least 9 books in 10 years
events.significance: Crisis reaches peak public awareness. Pering (1812) is the earliest book-length treatment, focused on ships. 1815 sees three books in a single year. The naval dimension (ships, dockyards) dominates — dry rot as national security issue during and after the Napoleonic Wars. These are advocacy documents, often with commercial interests in patented preservation treatments — read sceptically.

events.description: Samuel Pepys gathers toadstools 'as big as my Fists' from Royal Navy ship holds
events.significance: Earliest documented observation of fungal fruiting bodies on British naval timber. Conditions described (unventilated, damp, hot holds) indicate wet rot, not Serpula lacrymans. Pepys, as FRS and PRS, treats the type of decay as unsurprising — only the extent is shocking. Establishes baseline: 1680s naval timber decay is wet rot from neglect, not a novel invasive organism.

events.description: Conference of all eminent Master Builders in the River of Thames on timber quality
events.significance: Formal enquiry into causes of 30 new ships' decay. Rejected hastiness, green timber, and Baltic timber as causes. Endorsed foreign plank. Documented England's dependence on imported timber (5:1 ratio foreign to English). Results presented to King and Council Table.

events.description: Parliamentary programme of 30 new ships found catastrophically decayed through neglect
events.significance: Ships built at cost of £670,000+ found with timber 'perish'd to powder', sides disguised with boards and canvas. Several reported 'in danger of sinking at their very Moorings'. Pepys diagnosed cause as neglect during the 1679-1684 Commission period. The decay was wet rot, not dry rot.

events.description: Earl St Vincent runs down dockyard timber stocks while attacking corruption (1801-1804)
events.significance: Lambert identifies this as the ROOT CAUSE of the post-1805 dry rot explosion. St Vincent cancelled timber contracts, ran down seasoned stocks, and attacked the timber 'combination.' When war resumed, his successors were forced to accept worse contracts and green (unseasoned) timber. 'The use of green timber was the direct cause of the post-1805 explosion of dry rot.'

events.description: Captain Markham tells Commons merchant-built ships 'get what is called the dry rot' and have 'been the ruin of the navy'
events.significance: Earliest parliamentary use of 'dry rot' for SHIPS found in this research. Spoken during St Vincent's tenure. Explicitly blames merchant yards — the same yards forced to use green timber. Links dry rot to crew health ('inclined the men to fevers'). Fills the gap between the 1797 building crisis and the 1812 naval crisis literature.

events.description: Lionel Lukin's timber preservation kiln explodes at Woolwich dockyard, killing 6 workers
events.significance: Demonstrates the desperation of dry rot countermeasures by 1811. The Navy was willing to try dangerous industrial processes. Lukin survived and was subsequently sent with Sowerby to inspect the Queen Charlotte (1812).

events.description: The Times reports Queen Charlotte 'in a rapid state of decay, owing to the dry rot' — Canada oak blamed
events.significance: The moment the QC crisis became public news. The Times immediately identifies 'Canada oak' as the timber principally affected — establishing the public narrative that Canadian timber caused dry rot nine months before Sowerby's inspection. Confirms Knowles's claim that 'dry rot' was popularised by the QC discovery in 1811. The information was in the newspaper of record from the start.

events.description: The Times reports QC fumigation failed; total strip-down ordered; ship 'has never been at sea'
events.significance: Published 18 days before Sowerby's inspection (July 24). Documents the failure of chemical treatment and the decision to strip the ship — directly explaining why Sowerby found 'very little' B. lachrymans. The detail that the QC 'has never been at sea, except in the passage from Deptford to Plymouth' is the most devastating single sentence about the crisis: a first-rate warship rotted without ever serving.

events.description: Navy Board commissions James Sowerby F.L.S. to inspect HMS Queen Charlotte and advise on dry rot prevention
events.significance: FIRST DOCUMENTED CASE of a mycologist being hired by the Navy to diagnose ship dry rot using scientific taxonomy. The same man who illustrated B. lachrymans as 'Common Dry-rot' in 1797 is now deployed to the fleet. The commission letter explicitly frames the task as 'preventing the vegetation & spreading of the Fungi' — biological language, not miasmatic. The Navy Board had reached the point where mycological expertise was needed.

events.description: James Sowerby inspects HMS Queen Charlotte at Plymouth, identifies fungi by his own English Fungi plate numbers
events.significance: Sowerby found B. hybridus (EF 289), Xylostroma giganteum (EF 358), Auricularia pulverulenta (EF 214), and crucially 'very little remains of Boletus Lachrymans E.F. 113.' The ship was primarily attacked by OAK-SPECIFIC fungi, not the building dry rot organism. Hygrometer reading: 828.6° in hold vs 464.2° outside (on scale where 1000° = saturation). This is the first quantitative environmental measurement in the history of ship dry rot.

events.description: Admiralty adopts Sowerby's recommendations fleet-wide: ventilation, hygrometers, proper timber seasoning
events.significance: The Lords Commissioners of the Admiralty directed that Sowerby's measures 'shall be carried into execution in repairing that Ship as well as in building & repairing His Majesty's Ships at the several Yards.' Hygrometers to be 'used generally.' Report to be printed and circulated to officers of all yards. This made mycological science into FLEET POLICY for the first time.

events.description: Sowerby inspects Deptford and Woolwich dockyards with Commissioner Seppings, reports on timber stacking
events.significance: Extended Sowerby's role from ship inspection to dockyard infrastructure. Found timber at Deptford 'lying in a very uncomfortable & hazardous manner' — on marshy ground, too close together. Recommended raised platforms with arches, sheltered stacks with ventilation, hygrometer monitoring. Seppings identified practical difficulties; Sowerby proposed solutions. This was the transition from emergency ship repair to preventive timber management.

events.description: Sowerby commissioned to build 6 experimental timber stacks at Deptford dockyard under shelter with ventilation
events.significance: The Navy Board ordered Deptford to assist Sowerby in erecting 6 timber stacks 'housed over according to your plan.' This was a CONTROLLED EXPERIMENT in timber preservation — sheltered stacks with free air circulation, raised from ground, monitored by hygrometer. Connects to the postwar Byam Martin/Seppings programme of strategic timber seasoning reserves.

events.description: Navy Board awards Sowerby 200 guineas for dry rot services but denies credit for saving the Queen Charlotte
events.significance: The Navy Board awarded 200 guineas 'for the services which you have rendered in order to prevent as much as possible the alarming increase of the Dryrot in His Majesty's Ships; & to introduce an improved mode of seasoning & stowing away Timber.' But carefully noted: 'we do not attach to you the merit of having saved the Queen Charlotte by your advice, all the defective materials having been taken out of that Ship at Plymouth when she was repaired there.' Bureaucratic credit management — but the payment confirms the work was valued.

events.description: House of Lords Select Committee on Foreign Trade takes evidence on timber quality, dry rot, and Canadian vs European timber
events.significance: First of two parliamentary inquiries (1820 Lords, 1821 Commons). Triggered by petition from Baltic timber merchants against protective duties. Seppings's frigate durability data (Baltic 8 years vs Canadian 3-4 years) and Copland's building testimony became the evidentiary foundation for the free trade argument. Dry rot was weaponised as evidence that protectionism harmed consumers.

events.description: House of Commons Select Committee on Foreign Trade takes extensive evidence on timber and dry rot (184 pages, 45 dry rot mentions)
events.significance: THE MOST EXTENSIVE PRIMARY SOURCE on dry rot in the knowledge graph. Chaired by Thomas Wallace (Board of Trade). Expanded the witness base beyond London — Lancashire builders (Haigh, Bellhouse) CONTRADICTED the London consensus, reporting no dry rot in American timber and actual dry rot in Baltic timber. The geographic split suggests building practice (ventilation, wall construction) mattered more than timber species — consistent with Sowerby's 1812 emphasis on environment over material. The Russia Company Memorial framed dry rot as 'the previously rare disease' spread by protectionist policy. Committee recommended moderate tariff reduction. Bellhouse's description of fungus as 'strings like little bits of leather running all along in the joints' is a lay description of Serpula lacrymans rhizomorphs.

conceptual_frameworks.name: Miasmatic theory of decay
conceptual_frameworks.description: All organic decay caused by 'putrid, corroding air' — confined, damp air causes putrefaction of both flesh and timber
conceptual_frameworks.implications_for_dry_rot: Led to ventilation as primary remedy (accidentally correct for Serpula prevention) but prevented identification of biological cause

conceptual_frameworks.name: Antiseptic chemistry
conceptual_frameworks.description: Substances that resist putrefaction can preserve organic matter — salt, nitre, tannins, etc.
conceptual_frameworks.implications_for_dry_rot: Led to chemical treatments for timber preservation (tanning, painting, pitching) — some were accidentally effective anti-fungal agents

conceptual_frameworks.name: Marine worm damage
conceptual_frameworks.description: Ship hull destruction by Teredo worms — the dominant pre-1750 threat to ship timber
conceptual_frameworks.implications_for_dry_rot: The worm problem dominated ship preservation discourse; when a NEW kind of timber decay appeared (dry rot), it took time to distinguish from existing problems

KEY FINDING: dry rot as such is virtually absent from Phil Trans 1665-1869 despite the 1810s naval crisis. The Royal Society did not publish on the disease — the crisis literature appeared in standalone treatises (Pering, Knowles, McWilliam, Chapman) and parliamentary inquiries (1820 Lords, 1821 Commons), not in Phil Trans. This silence is itself a major historiographical datum.

Phil Trans full-text search across Pringle's presidency (1772-1778) finds no dry rot articles, only Mahon 1778's casual use of the term — undermining the 1823 Britannica claim that dry rot was 'known at some period of Sir John Pringle's presidency of the Royal Society'

Burke reaches for 'dry rot' to figure Jacobin internal subversion in Thoughts on French Affairs (composed Dec 1791, pub 1797). Earliest known metaphorical/political use. The figure's specific valence — hidden internal decay behind an intact-looking surface — is diagnostic for Serpula lacrymans rhizomorphic behaviour and presupposes lay familiarity with the phenomenon.

Burke was a sitting MP continuously 1765-1794: Wendover (1765-74), Bristol (1774-80), Malton (1780-94). He was therefore in the House of Commons in 1774 when 'dry rot' first appears in the HoC Journals as a timber term, and a sitting member for Malton in December 1791 when he composed the metaphorical use in Thoughts on French Affairs. The 1791 metaphor is drawn from the working vocabulary of the legislature, not borrowed from outside it.

Composed by a sitting MP. Closes the loop between the 1774 first-appearance of 'dry rot' in HoC Journals and the 1820-21 parliamentary inquiries: dry rot was discussed in and around the Commons throughout the half-century 1774-1821, and Burke's 1791 use sits at the midpoint.
Earliest newspaper report of QC decay. Identifies Canada oak as timber principally affected. Published Sept 19, 1811.
The Times report IS the public event — the moment the QC crisis entered public discourse.
Second Times report: fumigation failed, total strip-down, never been at sea. Published July 6, 18 days before Sowerby's inspection.
Documents the stripping that explains Sowerby's 'very little' B. lachrymans finding.
Sept 1811 discovery → fumigation attempt → July 1812 fumigation found to have failed → Sowerby commissioned July 17

Fumigation failed (Times July 6) → Navy Board commissions Sowerby (July 17) → inspection (July 24-25). The 18-day gap shows the Navy moved quickly once chemical treatment was publicly known to have failed.
Names QC among five rotten ships; reports Lukin received £5,000 for ineffectual treatment. Originally a Plymouth dispatch (Feb 17, 1818) also printed in The Times (Feb 20, 1818).
'Dr. Lukin, an Admiralty chemist, who received 5000l. for his ineffectual labours to stop the progress of vegetation in the ship.' Names both the person and the cost of failure.

modern_scholarship.citation: Kauserud, H. et al. (2007) 'Asian origin and rapid global spread of the destructive dry rot fungus Serpula lacrymans', Molecular Ecology 16(16): 3350-3360.
modern_scholarship.significance: Landmark phylogeographic study. Multiple gene genealogies demonstrate European indoor populations derived from Asian wild populations. Reduced genetic diversity in Europe consistent with founder event.

modern_scholarship.citation: Ramsbottom, J. (1937) 'Dry Rot in Ships', Essex Naturalist.
modern_scholarship.significance: Classic early academic treatment of the naval dry rot history. Should be consulted for primary source references.

modern_scholarship.citation: Ridout, B. (2000) Timber Decay in Buildings: The Conservation Approach to Treatment. London: E&FN Spon/Routledge.
modern_scholarship.significance: Provides historical context for understanding dry rot in British buildings.

modern_scholarship.citation: Oxford English Dictionary, entry for 'dry rot'
modern_scholarship.significance: Should contain the earliest documented uses with citations. Flagged as likely the most efficient way to verify our 1725/1774 findings.

open_questions.question: Does 'dry-rot' appear in Admiralty records, Navy Board minutes, or dockyard correspondence before 1778?
open_questions.significance: Would establish whether the term originated in naval or building contexts

open_questions.question: Does the Gentleman's Magazine or other 18th-century periodicals use the term before Mahon 1778?
open_questions.significance: Would narrow the date of entry into common usage

open_questions.question: What was the condition of Royal Navy ships laid up in ordinary in the 1760s-1780s?
open_questions.significance: Hales (1755) identifies the problem in harbour ships — Admiralty records would show whether it worsened
suggested_sources: Navy Board surveys of ships in ordinary

open_questions.question: Can we find builder's manuals or trade literature from 1750-1780 that discuss dry rot?
open_questions.significance: Mahon's casual use in 1778 implies builders already knew the term

open_questions.question: What timber was being used in British shipbuilding 1750-1800, and did sourcing change?
open_questions.significance: Imported Baltic timber (different species, different drying) may have been more susceptible to Serpula colonisation

open_questions.question: Are there parliamentary enquiries into naval timber quality before the well-known 1812 commission?
open_questions.significance: Would bridge the gap between Hales 1755 and the post-1800 crisis literature

open_questions.question: What is the relationship between the building construction boom of the 18th century and Serpula spread?
open_questions.significance: Mahon's 1778 reference is about buildings — was the construction boom creating ideal Serpula habitat?

open_questions.question: Can the European Magazine article (December 1811) describing the c.1798 Woolwich ship with 'orange and brown coloured fungi hanging in inverted cones' be located and verified?
open_questions.significance: Would confirm the earliest description of what sounds like Serpula lacrymans on a naval vessel. Currently known only through Britton 1875.

open_questions.question: What is the original source for Sir Joseph Banks's wine cask anecdote (fungi filling a cellar and lifting a cask)?
open_questions.significance: Would connect Banks directly to fungal growth observations. Britton 1875 cites it as 'recorded by Sir Joseph Banks' but gives no source.
suggested_sources: Banks Papers at British Library/Natural History Museum

open_questions.question: Did David Hartley use the term 'dry rot' in his fireproofing publications (1770s)?
open_questions.significance: Hartley's fireproofing work (1774-1776) predates Mahon (1778). Mann's 1778 French source discusses Hartley's plates AND mentions dry rot. If Hartley used the term, it would predate both Mahon and Mann.

open_questions.question: What are the earliest uses of 'dry rot' in the Times Digital Archive before the 1785 Keate v. Adams case?
open_questions.significance: The Times archive is searchable — a systematic search could find earlier uses.

open_questions.question: Can we identify the specific fungi Britton distinguishes as Polyporus hybridus ('the dry rot of oak-built vessels') vs Merulius lachrymans ('the Dry Rot' of buildings)?
open_questions.significance: Britton implies different organisms attacked ships (oak) vs buildings (fir/pine). This two-organism model could explain why the building dry rot crisis and the naval dry rot crisis appear as separate threads in the evidence.
open_questions.status: SUBSTANTIALLY ANSWERED (April 2026). Boletus hybridus Sowerby (EF Tab. 289) = Fibroporia vaillantii (DC.) Parmasto 1968, confirmed by GBIF, MycoDB, Index Fungorum, MycoBank, and Petersen (1983) Mycotaxon 17: 148-230. Transfer chain: B. hybridus → Polyporus hybridus (Berkeley & Broome c.1860) → Poria hybrida (Cooke 1886) → heterotypic synonym of F. vaillantii. This is a brown-rot polypore of coniferous timber in order Polyporales — taxonomically remote from Serpula lacrymans (order Boletales). Previous KG entry suggesting Donkioporia expansa was INCORRECT — D. expansa has a separate basionym (Boletus expansus Desm. 1823). SUBSTRATE PARADOX: F. vaillantii is a conifer specialist, but ships used mixed timbers (oak frames + pine/fir decking/masts/joinery). Sowerby never explicitly says B. hybridus was on oak — just on the ship. The B98 full species list (~25 species) confirms B. lachrymans was 'seldom' found. Sowerby's own draft (B98 p.16) explicitly distinguishes ship fungi (B. hybridus + Xylostroma) from B. lachrymans 'found more in damp building on shore.' REMAINING OPEN: no molecular study has re-examined Sowerby's specimens or tested historic ship timber. The synonymy rests on morphological convention (Petersen 1983), not molecular verification.

open_questions.question: What do Sowerby's field notebooks (NHM B96, 47 pages) and rough notes (B97, 152 pages) contain beyond the formal correspondence?
open_questions.significance: ANSWERED (April 2026). All documents fully read and analyzed.
open_questions.status: ANSWERED. Key findings: (1) B96 field notebook contains NO species identifications — Sowerby identified fungi retrospectively in London, not in the field. Contains travel diary, timber stacking sketches, tannin notes, Harmattan wind observation. (2) B97 earliest QC draft shows uncertainty: 'perhaps Boletus hybridus' and 'M. Lachrymans Dry rot?' with question mark. Contains humidity-fungi scale, destructiveness ranking, financial records, and categorized dockyard fungi list. (3) B98 contains the full ~25 species inventory (vs 4 in formal report), Sowerby's own two-organism distinction, and the resolved page 57 textual variant. (4) B95 reveals Knowles initiated the commission, Lukin's heating method was overridden by Admiralty, HMS Vigo also rotted under Lukin's plan, and Lukin asked whether QC was built of 'American Timber.'

open_questions.question: What was the relationship between Sowerby's 1812 recommendations and the postwar Byam Martin/Seppings programme?
open_questions.significance: Sowerby's experimental timber stacks at Deptford (1813) and his recommendations for seasoning under shelter prefigure the postwar programme described by Lambert. Did Byam Martin explicitly build on Sowerby's work? Were the Deptford stacks still in use after 1815?

open_questions.question: Why did the Navy Board deny Sowerby credit for saving the Queen Charlotte?
open_questions.significance: PARTIALLY ANSWERED (April 2026). The Jan 1814 payment letter states 'we do not attach to you the merit of having saved the Queen Charlotte.' But Sowerby's account claims she was 'providentially saved by my directions.'
open_questions.status: PARTIALLY ANSWERED. B95 correspondence reveals institutional dynamics: (1) Knowles and Seppings advised Sowerby to submit a memorial for payment — it was their idea, not his. (2) The Admiralty had already overridden Lukin's approach in favour of Sowerby's — the Board may have been managing Lukin's dignity. (3) The Navy Board credited Sowerby for 'seasoning & stowing away Timber' (the generalizable contribution) but not for saving one specific ship (which could have been attributed to the Plymouth repair crew). (4) By 1817, Seppings was asking for Sowerby's advice privately 'up the private stairs' but not paying — suggesting the relationship soured over credit and compensation. Sowerby refused to give further free advice. Pattern: the institution absorbed the expert's contribution while managing the credit attribution to protect hierarchies.

open_questions.question: Was HMS Queen Charlotte built of American timber, and what specific timber species were used?
open_questions.significance: Lukin's letter (8 Oct 1812) asks: 'I think that you told me yesterday that the Queen Charlotte was built of American Timber — was it so?' If built of American timber (which could include both American oak and American pine/fir), this changes the substrate ecology: F. vaillantii could have been on American softwood components, and the oak may have been chemically different from English oak. The answer to this question could resolve the substrate paradox for B. hybridus = F. vaillantii.

open_questions.question: What happened to Sowerby's dockyard fungi specimens?
open_questions.significance: Sowerby identified ~25 species from the dockyards. His herbarium specimens, if they survive at the Natural History Museum, could be subjected to DNA extraction and molecular identification — resolving the B. hybridus synonymy definitively. Sowerby's English Fungi illustrations (Tab. 289 etc.) are the type material for several species.

open_questions.question: What was the fate of HMS Vigo, and did Lukin's heating method cause or accelerate the dry rot?
open_questions.significance: Knowles (B95) reports that HMS Vigo, 'a ship built about three years since and heated according to Mr Lukins plan, is in a bad state from the dry rot.' This is evidence that Lukin's artificial heating approach — the method Sowerby opposed — was actively destructive. Did the Vigo's rot accelerate because stove heating raised humidity without ventilation, creating ideal conditions for fungal growth?
suggested_sources: Navy Board records on HMS Vigo

open_questions.question: Who was the 'Chapman' cited as principal authority (9 mentions) in the 1823 Britannica DRY-ROT article, and on what unpublished or contemporary source was the article based?
open_questions.significance: The 1823 DRY-ROT article was reprinted nearly verbatim through 1842 and 1860 — never revised. Identifying Chapman and the source materials would establish whose voice was institutionalized as the canonical British account of dry rot for nearly four decades. Candidates include William Chapman the civil engineer (1749-1832), who wrote on canal and dock works, or possibly a Royal Navy officer/surveyor of that name.

open_questions.question: Why did Britannica's NATURAL HISTORY article (1778) describe fungal succession on dead wood without anyone connecting it to timber decay for 45 years?
open_questions.significance: The ecological knowledge was present in the encyclopedia from 1778: liverworts → fungi → beetles → woodpeckers as a wood-decay succession. The same encyclopedia did not connect this to timber failure until DRY-ROT 1823. This is a case study in editorial siloing: knowledge that would have illuminated the naval crisis sat in the wrong article. Could any 1770s-1810s naval or building author have read NATURAL HISTORY and made the connection earlier?
suggested_sources: Citation analysis of Britannica NATURAL HISTORY article

institutions.name: Parliament of Great Britain / United Kingdom
institutions.dry_rot_connection: Parliament is the institutional thread that runs through the entire dry rot story from 1774 to 1821. The term 'dry rot' first appears in the House of Commons Journals in 1774 (church floors). Parliamentary committees were appointed to consider naval timber supply in 1771 and 1791. Burke was a sitting MP when he composed his 1791 metaphor. The 1820 House of Lords Select Committee on Timber and the 1821 House of Commons Select Committee on Timber are the central documentary site of the assemblage's political moment, where dry rot was weaponised in the Canadian-tariff debate. Parliament is therefore both a passive recorder of the term's circulation (Journals, debates) and an active producer of crisis discourse (committees, testimony, reports).
key_events_in_kg: 1774 — first appearance of 'dry rot' in HoC Journals as a timber term (church floors)
1771 — Committee appointed to consider how His Majesty's Navy may be better supplied with timber
1791 — Burke (sitting MP for Malton) composes Thoughts on French Affairs with the dry rot metaphor
1804 — Captain Markham's Commons speech on merchant-built ships ('the ruin of the navy')
1820 — House of Lords Select Committee on Timber (111 pp evidence)
1821 — House of Commons Select Committee on Timber (184 pp evidence)
